title: Add tiered batching dashboards (latency/queue/batch size) with cardinality-safe dimensions
preview: Summary: This PR adds/updates Grafana dashboards to support the tiered batching defaults launch (fast/standard/cost-efficient). The goal is to make it easy to answer: (1) which tier is active and why, (2) whether latency SLOs are being met per tier, (3) whether we are building unsafe queue depth / wait time under load, and (4) whether batch formation is behaving as expected (size and wait budget). Dashboards added: (A) Tiered Batching — Overview: high-level latency (p50/p95/p99), request rate, error rate, and saturation widgets with tier selector; includes a ‘canary vs control’ comparison row (for staged rollout) keyed off rollout cohort label when present. (B) Tiered Batching — Latency & Streaming: end-to-end latency and time-to-first-token panels, split by tier; includes separate panels for streaming vs non-streaming routes to avoid blending distributions. (C) Tiered Batching — Queue & Backpressure: queue depth, queue wait time, admission control rejects, shed/load-shed counts, and scheduler overload mode indicator (when enabled). (D) Tiered Batching — Batch Formation: batch_size_tokens histogram heatmap, batch_size_requests distribution, max_wait_ms utilization, and prefill/decode occupancy (when exported). (E) Tiered Batching — Regression Triage: side-by-side ‘current vs baseline’ p95/p99 deltas for top models, plus a panel for top offenders by (tier, model_family, region). Metrics and tags: This PR assumes runtime emits a tier label and reason codes per ENG-18426 / PR-28473; dashboards are built to work even when reason codes are missing (panels gracefully hide that row). Cardinality constraints are enforced by default: panels use model_family and route_group where possible; model is a template var with a safe allowlist and defaults to ‘top 10 by traffic’. Region is a template var but panels aggregate to region_group when high-cardinality environments are detected. Also adds guidance text within dashboards: “If you need per-route debugging, switch to the Route Debug dashboard and pin a single route to avoid unbounded series.” Alert readiness: No paging alerts are added in this PR (those live in alert-rules repo), but queries were shaped to be portable to PromQL recording rules. Includes draft threshold guidance in dashboard annotations (e.g., fast tier p99 regression > 10% for 15m at constant QPS) aligned with the SRE rollout checklist. Notable changes since first draft: (1) removed direct breakdown by tenant_id and full model name from the default view after review; (2) added a ‘series count’ panel to quickly spot label explosions; (3) renamed panels to match the SLO tier naming in the customer-facing docs (fast/standard/cost-efficient). Testing/validation: Exported dashboards loaded into staging Grafana; verified query performance under typical prod cardinality by replaying a 30m window in the metrics sandbox. Confirmed that all panels render with only tier labels enabled (no reason codes) and do not error when streaming labels are absent. Related: runtime metrics tier labels (redwood PR-28492), reason codes (redwood PR-28473), rollout flagging (redwood PR-28480), internal ticket INT-33012 (missing tier breakdown labels) resolved by aligning label keys used in dashboards with the runtime’s final taxonomy.

title: Add metering drift Grafana dashboard + alert rules (drift, freshness, duplicates)
preview:
Context
We’re standardizing a metering data-quality view so Finance/RevOps + Eng can quickly answer: (a) are usage events in sync with the billing ledger, (b) is data arriving on time for close/invoice previews, and (c) are we seeing duplicate usage events consistent with idempotency regressions/backfills.

This PR adds a Grafana dashboard (and initial alerting rules) for metering drift, freshness, and duplicate rates. It is intended to pair with the in-product Console sanity dashboard work (ENG-4415) and the scheduled drift job in the redwood monorepo (ENG-4421).

What’s included
- Dashboard: "Metering Sanity: Drift + Freshness" (folder: Billing/Metering)
  - Drift panels (usage_events vs billing_ledger aggregates)
    - Drift % by day (global)
    - Top-N accounts by absolute drift ($ and tokens) over last 24h / 7d
    - Drift by dimension: region, model, endpoint (chat/completions/embeddings)
    - Breakdown of drift causes when available (late_events, missing_dims, duplicates) from the drift job’s tagged metrics
  - Freshness panels
    - p50/p95 ingestion lag for usage_events (event_time -> available_in_warehouse)
    - Latest partition timestamps for key tables/exports (usage_events, usage_rollups, ledger_export)
    - "Freeze window" annotations (month-end close) based on the Finance agreed schedule
  - Duplicate rate panels
    - Duplicate usage_event_id rate (raw)
    - Duplicate dedupe_key collision rate (post-dedupe)
    - Retries/fallback signal overlays (when emitted) to correlate spikes

- Alerts (initial thresholds; tuned to avoid paging on small-account noise)
  - MeterDriftHigh (page): 24h drift % > 0.50% AND absolute drift > $2,000 OR > 5M billed_tokens, scoped per (region, endpoint)
  - MeterDriftModerate (ticket): 24h drift % > 0.25% AND absolute drift > $500
  - MeterFreshnessSLOBreach (page): p95 ingestion lag > 90 minutes for 30 minutes
  - MeterDuplicateRateHigh (page): duplicates > 0.10% of events for 20 minutes OR sustained > 0.05% for 2 hours
  - All alerts include runbook links + "known incident" annotation guidance

Metrics / data sources
- Prometheus series produced by drift job (redwood):
  - metering_drift_ratio{dimension=..., region=..., endpoint=...}
  - metering_drift_absolute_usd{...}
  - metering_drift_absolute_tokens{...}
  - metering_usage_ingestion_lag_seconds{quantile="0.95"}
  - metering_duplicate_rate{type="event_id"|"dedupe_key"}

Notes:
- This PR does not change metering logic. It only wires dashboard panels and rules to existing signals emitted by the job/export pipeline.
- For accounts with very small spend, percentage drift is noisy. Alerts gate on both % and absolute magnitude.

Rollout plan
1) Merge with alerts disabled by default (rule groups present; routing muted in prod for 48h).
2) Observe baseline noise during ongoing shadow billing compare.
3) Enable Moderate alerts to ticket first; then enable paging on DriftHigh + Freshness after threshold tuning.

Follow-ups
- Add a dedicated "embeddings batch" panel once ENG-4412 backfill completes (missing cache-hit/attribution fields).
- Add alert label for "meter_version" once shadow-billing compare exports meter_version consistently.

Screenshots
- Not included in PR (dashboards are generated from jsonnet). Use the rendered preview link in CI artifacts.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4421 (alert on meter drift), ENG-4415 (Console dashboard)
- Slack threads: meter drift alert tuning, freshness SLO discussion
- Runbooks: Confluence runbook will be linked once published (placeholder link included).

title: Tiered SLO dashboards + deploy/incident annotations (Hosted vs Dedicated views)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a tiered SLO dashboard set to the observability-pack, including (1) Hosted SLO overview, (2) Dedicated SLO overview (tier-wide; no customer-level by default), and (3) a Dedicated vs Hosted comparison view. It also adds Grafana annotations for deploys and incidents so we can correlate latency spikes / streaming disconnects / 5xx bursts with changes and incident timelines. Motivation: Part of the Q1 reliability milestone to formalize tiered SLOs + error budgets, we need canonical, tier-aware views that match the new SLI tagging at ingress and avoid metric cardinality blowups. This PR is intended to be the “default” internal dashboard surface linked from burn-rate alerts and runbooks. Out of scope: customer-facing Dedicated per-account dashboards and access control (tracked in ENG-4946 follow-ups) and any changes to SLI definitions/recording rules (those land in the monorepo + telemetry repos). Dependencies/assumptions: Requires tier/deployment mode labels to be present on the primary request/latency/streaming metrics (see redwood PR-28411) and burn-rate recording rules to exist (redwood PR-28492). Key changes: (A) New dashboards: 1) SLO Overview — Hosted (tier=hosted, region filter, endpoint_class filter; panels for availability/success rate, p50/p95/p99 latency, streaming disconnect rate, and top contributors by endpoint_class + region). 2) SLO Overview — Dedicated (tier=dedicated; same core panels; includes headroom hints panel that links to fleet dashboards but does not embed per-customer dimensions). 3) SLO Compare — Hosted vs Dedicated (side-by-side availability, latency percentiles, and streaming reliability; uses a shared time range + shared region selection; intended for incident review and target negotiation with PM). (B) Annotation tracks: “Deploys” annotation fed by deploy event series (redwood_deploy_events_total + build SHA label, aggregated to avoid per-service noise), “Incidents” annotation fed by incident-bot exported events (incident_events_total) with severity and incident_id, and a “Budget burn state” annotation derived from burn-rate alert firing series for quick visual overlay. (C) Dashboard conventions: standard variables (region, endpoint_class, tier), consistent SLO links in panel descriptions, and a top-of-dashboard note on SLI exclusions (invalid requests, explicit rate limits, client aborts) to reduce confusion when support/PM teams compare dashboards with raw logs. (D) Cardinality guardrails: no per-model and no per-account breakdowns in the default dashboard; top contributors are capped to N=10 and use endpoint_class + route_group only (route_group is derived/normalized by recording rules). A hidden “__debug” toggle exists for SRE-only use to reveal model breakdown if the underlying metrics are safe; default is off. Validation: ran grafana-lint + jsonnet compilation; loaded dashboards into staging Grafana and verified variables resolve for us-east-1/eu-west-1; spot-checked that streaming disconnect panel does not include client aborts per current metric taxonomy. Screenshots: included in the PR conversation (staging links). Rollout: once merged, this pack is picked up by the weekly observability-pack sync job; SRE will pin alert runbook links to the new dashboard UIDs after deploy.

title: Add FP8/INT8 benchmark + rollout Grafana dashboards and canary alerting
preview:
## Context
Serving Runtime is running an FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking + staged rollout sprint. SRE/Runtime asked for a single place to monitor benchmark runs (offline) and the perf-canary rollout (online) with consistent dimensions.

This PR adds a small dashboard pack + alert rules in `observability-pack`:
- Grafana dashboards for benchmark runs and rollout monitoring
- Prometheus recording rules to normalize TTFT/TPOT/tokens/s aggregations and reduce query cost
- Alert rules intended for perf canary gating (and incident-style triage)

## What’s included
### 1) Dashboard: **FP8/INT8 – Benchmark Runs**
Folder: `Serving Runtime / Performance`
Primary purpose: benchmark execution monitoring + sanity checks while running the matrix.
Panels:
- TTFT (ms) p50/p95 by `model`, `gpu_sku`, `kernel_mode` (baseline|fp8|int8), `region`
- TPOT (ms/token) p50/p95 (decode-heavy visibility)
- Tokens/s (effective) and requests/s
- Error rate + error codes (runtime error class breakdown)
- GPU util (%), SM active (% if exported), GPU memory used/total, OOM counters
- Scheduler breakdown panels (if present): prefill vs decode time share, queue depth p95

Benchmark-specific UX:
- Requires `bench_run_id` label when available; otherwise filters by `job` + `git_sha`.
- Adds an annotation query for rollout toggles (if `kernel_mode` flag events are shipped; optional).

### 2) Dashboard: **FP8/INT8 – Rollout / Perf Canary**
Primary purpose: compare canary cohort vs baseline cohort during staged enablement.
Panels:
- Canary vs baseline overlays for TTFT p95, TPOT p95, tokens/s p50 (time-aligned)
- Heatmap panels (TTFT buckets) to catch tail regressions masked by p95
- Slice-by tables (top regressions) grouped by `model`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `kernel_mode`
- Error budget quick-view: error rate, timeout rate, fallback rate (where emitted)
- GPU saturation + memory headroom (to detect scheduler/packing changes masquerading as kernel wins)

### 3) Dashboard: **FP8/INT8 – GPU Health (Perf Context)**
Primary purpose: quickly rule out “it’s the fleet” during noisy runs.
Panels:
- GPU clocks (when exported), thermal throttle counters (if present), GPU util and mem
- Node-level saturation and queue depth correlation
- Error spikes correlated with GPU OOM / CUDA errors

## Metrics and dimensions
This PR assumes the runtime metrics are tagged with (at minimum):
- `model`
- `gpu_sku`
- `region`
- `kernel_mode` (baseline|fp8|int8)

Where available, dashboards also use:
- `cohort` (baseline|canary) for perf-canary comparisons
- `prefill_decode` (prefill|decode) for scheduler splits

If any of these labels are missing, dashboards will still render but lose key filtering/overlays.

## Alerting (perf canary)
New alerts are scoped to the perf-canary use case (fast detection + low false positives). Summary:
- **PerfCanaryTTFTP95Regression**: TTFT p95 regression >= 8% vs baseline for 10m, gated on minimum sample size.
- **PerfCanaryTPOTP95Regression**: TPOT p95 regression >= 6% vs baseline for 10m, gated on minimum sample size.
- **PerfCanaryTokensPerSecDrop**: tokens/s p50 drop >= 7% vs baseline for 15m.
- **PerfCanaryErrorRateSpike**: error rate > 0.5% for 5m (or +3x vs baseline where comparable).
- **PerfCanaryOOMSpike**: GPU OOM counter increase above threshold.

Noise suppression:
- Recording rules compute rolling rates and quantiles on fixed windows.
- All perf-regression alerts include a `min_requests` gate to avoid small-sample false positives (feedback from #eng-sre).

Routing:
- Alerts default to `#eng-sre` / `#eng-runtime` (per existing alertmanager routing for the folder). No paging is configured by default; SRE can promote to paging per rollout.

## How to test
- `make grafana-render` (renders jsonnet → dashboard JSON)
- `make grafana-lint` (format + schema checks)
- `promtool test rules prometheus/tests/fp8_int8_alerts_test.yaml`

## Rollout / compatibility notes
- No changes to metric emission in this repo.
- If `kernel_mode` isn’t present yet in a given cluster, tables/overlays will show “(missing)” and queries will collapse across modes.
- The canary dashboard expects `cohort` labels if perf-canary is emitting them; otherwise use the `region` + `kernel_mode` filters.

## Screenshots
Attached in PR artifacts: benchmark overview (TTFT/TPOT), canary overlay, and slice-by table examples.

## Review notes / decision points
- Threshold defaults are intentionally conservative. Rafael requested we start with non-paging alerts and tighten once we see real noise levels.
- Marcus requested scheduler queue depth + prefill/decode split panels, included in this revision (panels gracefully hide if missing).

## Related
- Linear: ENG-7427 (dashboards), ENG-7426 (canary thresholds)
- Runtime PRs: metrics tagging for model/gpu/mode is tracked separately; this pack assumes those labels exist.

---
### Timeline
- 2026-02-13: Initial dashboard scaffolding + folder wiring
- 2026-02-16: Added recording rules + first alert pass
- 2026-02-18: Noise suppression + min-sample gating; added scheduler split panels
- 2026-02-19: Final review, promtool tests, merged

title: Add Suggestions API + funnel Grafana dashboards and beta alerting
preview: Summary: Adds a first-pass set of Grafana dashboards + alert rules for the Optimization Suggestions beta, covering (1) Suggestions API health (latency/errors/payload) and (2) the Suggestions view funnel (impression → expand → dismiss → apply-click), including breakdowns by org/model/route/region with conservative cardinality controls. Motivation: during dogfood we had a p95 regression + early customer reports of “empty despite traffic” and “estimates look off”; we need a single place for SRE/Platform + product to validate the pipeline (compute freshness), the API (error/latency), and the UX funnel (are people seeing/using suggestions). Scope notes: This PR only adds dashboards/alerts in observability-pack; it assumes the backend/UI already emits the metrics/events (ENG-19402) and the Suggestions API exists (ENG-19341). No changes to application code here. Dashboards added: (A) Optimize / Suggestions API Health: request rate, p50/p95 latency, 4xx/5xx, top error codes, payload size, cache hit/miss (if available), and per-route hot spots. Includes links to the runbook for triage. (B) Optimize / Suggestions Funnel (Beta): counts + conversion rates for suggestions_impression, suggestions_row_expand, suggestions_dismiss, suggestions_apply_click; time series + cohort table, with optional filters (org_id, project_id, model, route, region) and a “minimum volume” guardrail. (C) Optimize / Suggestions Compute Freshness: staleness/lag from the compute job (stale_as_of age), recompute success/failure, and lag percentiles so support can tell if “insufficient telemetry” is real vs pipeline delay. Alerting: Adds lightweight beta alert rules with conservative thresholds and paging only on clear system issues. SuggestionsAPIHigh5xxRate (page): 5xx > 1% for 10m (per env) with a minimum request volume gate. SuggestionsAPIP95LatencyHigh (page): p95 > 350ms for 15m (same volume gate). SuggestionsComputeLagHigh (page): compute freshness lag p95 > 6h for 30m. SuggestionsEventIngestionDrop (ticket): impressions drop to ~0 while API request volume is non-zero (helps catch client-side event pipeline breaks). All alerts include runbook links and labels for routing to eng-sre/eng-platform. Privacy/cardinality: Funnel breakdowns use org_id/project_id (numeric/UUID) and do not include org name; we cap table panels to top-N by volume and add a $__min_volume variable to reduce noisy / low-signal slices. Notes for rollout: Dashboards are safe to merge before the beta flag expands; alerts should be enabled in staging immediately and in prod with “ticket” severity for 24h before paging (see checklist).

title: Audit log pipeline dashboards + alerts (ingestion latency, drop rate) with runbook links
preview:
Context: As part of the audit logging standardization effort we’re tightening operational visibility for the audit event ingestion/export path. We’ve had a couple of near-misses where ingestion lag spiked without a clear on-call landing page, and early alert drafts lacked enough context to triage quickly.

This PR adds:
- A Grafana dashboard for the audit-log ingestion pipeline (end-to-end delivery latency, ingest backlog, drop rate, retry/429/5xx, DLQ growth).
- Alert rules for delivery latency and drop rate with sane defaults + per-environment routing.
- Runbook links embedded in alert annotations and dashboard panel descriptions.

Key signals / definitions:
- delivery_latency_seconds: time from event emission at producer to ack at ingestion sink (measured via audit-ingest gateway metrics + exporter timestamps).
- drop_rate: fraction of events not successfully delivered after retry budget (from ingest and exporter counters).
- backlog: queued events in buffer (when enabled) + ingestion consumer lag.

Alert thresholds (initial):
- AuditLogDeliveryLatencyP99High: p99(delivery_latency_seconds) > 60s for 10m (page: enterprise tiers; ticket: others).
- AuditLogDropRateHigh: drop_rate > 0.10% for 5m (page; includes per-region breakdown).
- AuditLogIngestBacklogGrowing: backlog > 50k AND increasing for 15m (ticket; pages if > 200k).

Cardinality guardrails:
- Dashboard panels group primarily by region, deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private), and service. We intentionally do not group by raw tenant_id/project_id. event_type is only used in a single table panel with top-N + allow-listed types.

Runbooks:
- Incident runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/audit-log-pipeline-incident-runbook.json
- Coverage regression runbook is linked from the dashboard “Coverage” row for follow-up when events are missing but pipeline is healthy.

Testing:
- jsonnetfmt + dashboards render in CI.
- promtool rules tests added for the new alert expressions.

Notes:
- This is intended to be the “default landing page” linked from paging alerts. We can iterate thresholds after a week of production data; see INT-3984 for noise tuning follow-ups.

title: Quality gates: dashboards + alert rules (pass rate, failure spikes, latency, flake rate)
preview: Adds first-class observability for Optimize quality gate runs. This PR introduces (1) a Grafana dashboard for the full gate-run funnel (created → running → passed/failed/overridden), (2) Prometheus alert rules for gate failure spikes, flake rate regressions, and gate execution latency/SLO breaches, and (3) runbook links + consistent labels so we can slice by config_type (batching/cache/quant_profile), model_family, route_id, account_tier, and region. Motivation: we’re rolling out mandatory gating ahead of applying Optimize recommendations; we need to catch (a) real quality regressions, (b) eval-harness instability/flaky suites causing false blocks, and (c) infrastructure constraints causing timeouts (notably EU-West). Design notes: alerts avoid paging on single-run failures; they trigger on burn-rate / sustained increases and include a ‘possible flake’ annotation when rerun aggregation indicates high variance. Dashboard includes deep links back into Console (gate run id) and the gate failure runbook for oncall. This aligns metric names/tags with the quality-gates observability standard and uses the new gate_run_id trace correlation field emitted by the Optimize service. Rollout: rules are initially deployed in ‘warning’ severity for 1 week; paging thresholds are enabled after we confirm baseline noise levels in limited production rollout.

title: Add SDK retry/timeout/streaming disconnect dashboard panels + rollout alerts
preview:
Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity rollout (standard retries/backoff, timeout model, and streaming disconnect handling), SRE requested first-class dashboards and conservative alerts to monitor load impact and customer-facing failure rates during beta/stable releases. This PR adds (1) new panels to the existing "SDK Client Health" Grafana dashboard for retry rate, timeout rate, and streaming disconnect reasons, (2) companion alert rules with rollout-friendly thresholds and burn-rate style paging, and (3) a small set of recording rules to make the queries cheaper/consistent across environments.

What’s included: - Dashboard panels: Retry rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, model, status_code), Retry attempts distribution (p50/p95 of retry_count per request), Timeout rate (by sdk_language, timeout_scope=overall|connect|read), Timeout duration (histogram heatmap where available), Streaming disconnect rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, reason), Top streaming disconnect reasons (table), and a rollout view grouped by sdk_version (when the label is present). - Alerts (disabled-by-default in prod, enabled in beta + dogfood): (A) High SDK retry rate (warn), (B) High SDK timeout rate (warn), (C) Streaming disconnect spike (warn), (D) Potential retry storm indicator (page) using (retry_total / request_total) + 429 rate + concurrency as gates. - Recording rules: pre-aggregate sdk_client_request_total, sdk_client_retry_total, sdk_client_timeout_total, sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total into 5m rate series with a bounded label set.

Metrics used (existing / recently standardized fields): - sdk_client_request_total{sdk_language, sdk_version?, endpoint, model, status_code} - sdk_client_retry_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_timeout_total{sdk_language, endpoint, timeout_scope} - sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_request_duration_seconds_bucket (optional; used when emitted)

Notes / assumptions: - Streaming disconnect reasons follow the parity taxonomy being rolled out in SDKs (e.g., server_fin, read_timeout, client_cancel, proxy_reset, tls_error, unknown). Panels will still render if reason is missing (falls into reason="unknown" in the dashboards). - To avoid alert noise from a small number of clients, alerts include a minimum volume gate (requests_per_min) and are scoped to the rollout dashboards folder. - This PR intentionally does NOT add new instrumentation; it only consumes the standardized fields being added in the SDK repos. If a given SDK release doesn’t yet include sdk_version label, the dashboard will still work (the sdk_version view will simply be empty).

Rollout plan: - Merge now; ship to dogfood + beta monitoring stacks. - Enable alerts in beta immediately; keep prod alerts muted until stable releases are out (target: after ENG-5131 go/no-go). - During the first week of stable: watch retry_rate, timeout_rate, and disconnect_reason mix for proxy_reset/read_timeout regressions; use request_id sampling from SDK logs to correlate.

Testing: - Rendered dashboards via jsonnet/grafonnet compile locally; validated queries against staging Prometheus with synthetic load. - Ran alert rule unit tests (promtool) with fixtures covering 429 spikes and elevated read timeouts.

Screenshots: Included in the PR conversation (Grafana panels: Retry rate by language, Streaming disconnect reasons table).

Follow-ups: - Add a dedicated panel for Retry-After compliance once the gateway header adoption is >90% (separate PR). - Consider a dashboard annotation hook for SDK release cut times (Connor coordinating in ENG-RELEASES).

title: Add audit log lag dashboard + alerts and wire runbook links (ENG-4824)
preview:
Context: Support + CS escalations (notably around audit log delivery latency) highlighted that we lacked a first-class, Support/CS-friendly view of end-to-end audit log pipeline lag and an alert that aligns to enterprise expectations. This PR adds a dedicated dashboard and alerting rules for audit log lag, and ensures panels/alerts link to a canonical runbook. It is intended to reduce time-to-triage during tickets like SUP-1846 and to support the known-issues sprint playbook work by providing an authoritative signal.

What’s included: (1) New Grafana dashboard: "Audit Logs — Delivery Lag" with panels for ingestion → transform → delivery stages, p50/p95/p99 lag, backlog depth (where available), per-region breakdown, and a quick "is this customer-impacting" section (current lag vs expected SLO). (2) New Prometheus alerts: audit-log-lag warning + critical, scoped to production namespaces and grouped by region, with annotations that link directly to the runbook and dashboard. (3) Runbook updates: new section for audit log lag triage, plus explicit "Support-facing guidance" (what we can safely confirm, what data we should avoid sharing, and what mitigation knobs exist). (4) Routing/ownership: alerts are routed to SRE on-call; annotations include escalation guidance for Platform ownership boundaries.

Alert thresholds (aligned to enterprise expectations): - Warning: p95 delivery lag > 10m for 10m (per region) - Critical: p95 delivery lag > 30m for 10m (per region) Notes: These thresholds intentionally do not page on transient spikes; they aim to catch sustained backlog that will breach enterprise expectations. Runbook describes how to validate whether this is isolated to a subset of tenants vs global/regional.

Key design choices: - Use p95 as the primary paging signal to avoid single-tenant tail behavior dominating. Dashboard still shows p99 for investigation. - Region grouping matches how Support/CS communicate impact (us-east/eu-west/etc.) and how status events are typically framed. - Runbook contains customer-safe language; no tenant identifiers or customer names are present in dashboard queries.

Testing/validation: - Verified dashboard JSON imports cleanly into staging Grafana and renders for both "prod" and "staging" datasources. - Verified alert rule evaluation against a replayed backlog scenario (staging) triggers warning/critical as expected and resolves cleanly. - Verified links in alert annotations resolve (runbook, dashboard, and on-call channel).

Rollout: - Merge to main + apply via standard observability-pack deploy pipeline (next scheduled deploy window). - SRE on-call to monitor for noisy alerts during first 48 hours; if noisy, adjust the for-duration before adjusting thresholds.

Screenshots: Included in PR checks artifacts (Grafana render export) for quick review.

Follow-ups (out of scope for this PR): - Add a dedicated metric for per-tenant lag that can be surfaced only in restricted dashboards (requires security review). - Add an explicit "expected delivery SLO" panel driven by tier metadata (would require joining tier labels into metrics).

title: Add pseudonymous token-cost summarizer and privacy-hash bridge
preview: Context/Motivation: We need per-invocation token cost visibility without retaining plaintext prompt or response tokens in observability backends. This PR introduces a privacy-first pipeline: (1) deterministic, salted token-hash bucketing at the edge; (2) a compact per-invocation cost summarizer that attributes per-bucket token counts and tokenized-costs; (3) a bridge service that joins hashed token buckets with model cost profiles for rollups and dashboards. Goals: provide accurate cost attribution for SLOs and billing heuristics while ensuring PII/PHI is never logged or exported. Changes are designed to be reversible (config toggles) and auditable. Main changes: - collector: add pseudonymization middleware that applies a per-tenant salt and BLAKE3-based chunked hashing to token fragments, exposing only bucket_id and hash_prefix_size metadata (no token text). - summarizer: a streaming aggregator that emits per-invocation compact summaries: total_tokens, generation_tokens, prompt_token_buckets (list of bucket_id:count), and model_cost_approx_usd (aggregated using model profile). - bridge service: lightweight joiner that maps bucket-level aggregates to model cost model to compute per-route and per-tenant cost metrics used by dashboards/alerts. - dashboards: add a new Observability Pack dashboard panel group 'Pseudonymous Cost Explorer' with per-route aggregated cost and a new 'quiet-surge' alert rule that triggers only when cost growth is sustained across at least 3 hours and >75th percentile of bucket skew. - telemetry: add metrics and labels for pseudonymization_version, salt_id, and summarizer_latency_ms; tracing spans annotated with sample_rate and anonymized_bucket_count. Backwards compatibility: collector can run in dual-mode emitting both legacy plaintext token telemetry (for fast migration) and pseudonymized summaries behind a feature flag. Checklist: [x] design review (ENG-4321), [x] privacy review with security, [x] unit tests for hashing and summarizer, [x] integration test with bridge, [x] dashboard screens, [x] docs draft. Migrations/deploy: - rollout plan: enable pseudonymization off-by-default; stage enable on a small subset of tenants (canary 1%), monitor summarizer latency and cost deltas for 48h, then ramp to 100% over 5 days. - rollback plan: flip feature flag to legacy mode (no schema migration required). - data retention: summarized rows kept 90d; raw hashed metadata kept 7d and then rotated. Security/privacy notes: salts are tenant-scoped and stored in KMS-backed vault; hashing performed in-process so unhashed tokens never leave the collector process. Commit summary: 1) add privacy middleware and deterministic token hashing; 2) streaming summarizer service and proto changes for compact summary; 3) bridge joiner service and query adapters for dashboards; 4) observability pack dashboard panels and alert rule; 5) e2e tests and runbook. Key files changed: observability-pack/pkg/collector/pseudonymizer.go, observability-pack/pkg/summarizer/stream_aggregator.go, observability-pack/services/bridge/joiner.go, observability-pack/dashboards/pseudonymous_cost_explorer.jsonnet, observability-pack/alerts/quiet_surge.yaml, docs/observability/privacy-token-summarizer.md, test/e2e/pseudonymizer_integration_test.go, scripts/bench/summarizer_bench.go, go.mod, go.sum. Tests: added unit tests for hash stability and collision resistance, integration test that validates summarizer output vs synthetic tokenized inputs, benchmark for 99th-pctl summarizer latency. Observability/Alerting: - New metric: pseudonymizer.tokens_hashed_total{tenant,salt_id} - New histogram: summarizer.latency_ms - New alert: observability.quiet_surge_high_cost (suppresses noisy short spikes; logic included in alert yaml) Release notes (short): Adds a privacy-preserving token-cost summarizer that allows cost attribution without storing token text; dashboards and alerts submitted with observability pack. Rollout and monitoring instructions included. Rationale and alternatives considered: we evaluated full differential privacy vs deterministic hashing; deterministic salted hashing was chosen for lower operational complexity and deterministic joins. We added salt rotation support as a safety mechanism. Open questions: - how to present per-bucket explanations to end-users without leaking token content? We opted for statistical bucket descriptors (e.g., average token length, token_type_entropy) rather than examples. - future: consider integrating cryptographic PPE techniques for smaller tenants. Observability runbook excerpt: On spike after enabling, check summarizer.latency_ms, look for high anonymized_bucket_count, and if latency > 200ms for 5m trigger rollback. Compliance: privacy review approved; KMS usage logged.

This PR introduces a privacy-first pipeline: (1) deterministic, salted token-hash bucketing at the edge; (2) a compact per-invocation cost summarizer that attributes per-bucket token counts and tokenized-costs; (3) a bridge service that joins hashed token buckets with model cost profiles for rollups and dashboards. Goals: provide accurate cost attribution for SLOs and billing heuristics while ensuring PII/PHI is never logged or exported. Changes are designed to be reversible (config toggles) and auditable. Main changes: - collector: add pseudonymization middleware that applies a per-tenant salt and BLAKE3-based chunked hashing to token fragments, exposing only bucket_id and hash_prefix_size metadata (no token text). - summarizer: a streaming aggregator that emits per-invocation compact summaries: total_tokens, generation_tokens, prompt_token_buckets (list of bucket_id:count), and model_cost_approx_usd (aggregated using model profile). - bridge service: lightweight joiner that maps bucket-level aggregates to model cost model to compute per-route and per-tenant cost metrics used by dashboards/alerts. - dashboards: add a new Observability Pack dashboard panel group 'Pseudonymous Cost Explorer' with per-route aggregated cost and a new 'quiet-surge' alert rule that triggers only when cost growth is sustained across at least 3 hours and >75th percentile of bucket skew. - telemetry: add metrics and labels for pseudonymization_version, salt_id, and summarizer_latency_ms; tracing spans annotated with sample_rate and anonymized_bucket_count. Backwards compatibility: collector can run in dual-mode emitting both legacy plaintext token telemetry (for fast migration) and pseudonymized summaries behind a feature flag. Checklist: [x] design review (ENG-4321), [x] privacy review with security, [x] unit tests for hashing and summarizer, [x] integration test with bridge, [x] dashboard screens, [x] docs draft. Migrations/deploy: - rollout plan: enable pseudonymization off-by-default; stage enable on a small subset of tenants (canary 1%), monitor summarizer latency and cost deltas for 48h, then ramp to 100% over 5 days. - rollback plan: flip feature flag to legacy mode (no schema migration required). - data retention: summarized rows kept 90d; raw hashed metadata kept 7d and then rotated. Security/privacy notes: salts are tenant-scoped and stored in KMS-backed vault; hashing performed in-process so unhashed tokens never leave the collector process. Commit summary: 1) add privacy middleware and deterministic token hashing; 2) streaming summarizer service and proto changes for compact summary; 3) bridge joiner service and query adapters for dashboards; 4) observability pack dashboard panels and alert rule; 5) e2e tests and runbook. Key files changed: observability-pack/pkg/collector/pseudonymizer.go, observability-pack/pkg/summarizer/stream_aggregator.go, observability-pack/services/bridge/joiner.go, observability-pack/dashboards/pseudonymous_cost_explorer.jsonnet, observability-pack/alerts/quiet_surge.yaml, docs/observability/privacy-token-summarizer.md, test/e2e/pseudonymizer_integration_test.go, scripts/bench/summarizer_bench.go, go.mod, go.sum. Tests: added unit tests for hash stability and collision resistance, integration test that validates summarizer output vs synthetic tokenized inputs, benchmark for 99th-pctl summarizer latency. Observability/Alerting: - New metric: pseudonymizer.tokens_hashed_total{tenant,salt_id} - New histogram: summarizer.latency_ms - New alert: observability.quiet_surge_high_cost (suppresses noisy short spikes; logic included in alert yaml) Release notes (short): Adds a privacy-preserving token-cost summarizer that allows cost attribution without storing token text; dashboards and alerts submitted with observability pack. Rollout and monitoring instructions included. Rationale and alternatives considered: we evaluated full differential privacy vs deterministic hashing; deterministic salted hashing was chosen for lower operational complexity and deterministic joins. We added salt rotation support as a safety mechanism. Open questions: - how to present per-bucket explanations to end-users without leaking token content? We opted for statistical bucket descriptors (e.g., average token length, token_type_entropy) rather than examples. - future: consider integrating cryptographic PPE techniques for smaller tenants. Observability runbook excerpt: On spike after enabling, check summarizer.latency_ms, look for high anonymized_bucket_count, and if latency > 200ms for 5m trigger rollback. Compliance: privacy review approved; KMS usage logged.

title: perf-canary: add paging (Sev2/Sev1) alert rules for sustained per-model regressions
preview: Summary: This adds paging-tier perf-canary alert rules (Sev2 + Sev1) for sustained regressions on top models. These are intentionally stricter than the warning rules in pr-19179 (higher minimum sample size, longer sustain window, and stricter anti-flap) and route to the on-call rotation channel via incident-bot. Motivation: During phase-1 rollout we saw warning-level regressions that were actionable when they sustained (real tail-latency impact), but the existing rules were designed to be noisy-tolerant and non-paging. This PR implements the paging path that SRE approved in the paging policy thread and is required for rollout gates integration. Alerting model: We page only for top-model + sustained regressions where we have enough canary volume to reduce false positives, and we gate on a canary-health signal (telemetry freshness + request loop healthy) to avoid paging on broken canary. Key behaviors: 1) Severity levels: Sev2 (page) for sustained p95 latency regression OR sustained tokens/sec drop on top models; Sev1 (page) only if regression is severe and sustained across consecutive windows. 2) Attribution-friendly annotations: every alert includes model, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_tag (if present), region, hardware_sku, baseline id, observed vs baseline deltas, and deep links to the Grafana dashboard + runbook. 3) Anti-flap + dedupe: minimum sample size, sustain windows, and incident-bot dedupe keys based on (model, region, hardware_sku, signal, severity). 4) Routing: Sev2/Sev1 route to #eng-oncall and create an incident-bot thread tagged perf-canary; warning-level continues to #eng-runtime. Thresholds (initial defaults, tuned for us-east phase-1): p95_e2e_latency_ms regression: Sev2 if >= +15% over baseline for 20m AND samples >= 200 AND canary_health=ok; Sev1 if >= +30% for 30m AND samples >= 300 AND canary_health=ok. tokens_per_sec regression: Sev2 if <= -12% for 25m AND samples >= 150; Sev1 if <= -20% for 35m AND samples >= 250. Note: These are defaults; per-model overrides live in control-plane config and will be adjusted during threshold calibration. Rollout: These rules are initially scoped to top models only (via label matcher on canary_model_tier=top). They are disabled by default in non-prod; in prod they are deployed but will be set to "dry-run" (notify-only) for 48h in us-east until SRE sign-off, then flipped to paging. Testing/validation: - Unit: alert rule lint + promtool rules tests; - Integration: rendered rules in staging Grafana; - Manual: replayed recent canary time series (including known regression from SUP-3841) to confirm expected firing behavior and no false triggers on short spikes. Links: - Linear ENG-4878 (alerting policy + routing), ENG-4880 (phase-1 us-east rollout), PM-642 (rollout gates integration) - Runbook: confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert - Dashboard: grafana/perf-canary/per-model (added in pr-19174) - Prior art: pr-19179 (warning-level rules) Checklist: - [x] rules have sustain window + min sample size - [x] rules gated by canary health and telemetry freshness - [x] incident-bot routing + dedupe key added - [x] annotations include dashboard/runbook links - [x] promtool tests added - [x] reviewed by SRE for paging hygiene

title: Add Usage Insights freshness/quality dashboard + alerts (lag + reconciliation drift)
preview:
Motivation: Usage Insights is going into beta with interactive console queries backed by hourly/day partitions and reconciliation checks against billing. We need a standard dashboard + alert bundle in observability-pack so SRE/on-call can quickly answer: (1) is data fresh, (2) are we dropping/late-ingesting events by route/model, (3) are we drifting vs billing totals beyond tolerance, and (4) are query endpoints timing out for common ranges.

This PR adds a Grafana dashboard and alert rule templates for freshness lag, late event rate, reconciliation drift, and query p95 latency. The dashboard is designed to be imported into customer-facing/internal stacks with minimal per-cluster edits (templated variables for env/region/deployment_mode/workspace_id).

What’s included: 1) Dashboard: “Usage Insights — Data Freshness & Quality” (Grafana JSON) Panels: Aggregation lag (p50/p95) by dataset (route_model_hourly, route_model_daily) and by region; Ingestion late-event ratio (late_events / total_events) by region + streaming endpoint flag; Missing route_tag rate (events where route_template = "unknown" or null); Reconciliation drift (%) between insights totals and billing hook totals, per workspace and overall; Top offenders table: workspaces with highest drift and highest lag; Query API p95 latency + error rate (v1/usage-insights/* endpoints) with annotations for deploys.

2) Alerts (PrometheusRule templates): - UsageInsightsAggregationLagHigh: fires when p95 aggregation lag > 20m for 15m (warn) and > 60m for 10m (page). - UsageInsightsLateEventRatioHigh: fires when late_event_ratio > 2% for 30m (warn), with a separate page threshold at 5% for 15m. - UsageInsightsRouteTagMissing: fires when missing_route_tag_rate > 1% for 30m (warn) and > 3% for 15m (page). - UsageInsightsReconciliationDriftHigh: fires when abs(drift_pct) > 0.5% for 2h (warn) and > 1.0% for 1h (page), excluding small totals (< $50/day) to reduce noise. - UsageInsightsQueryP95Degraded: fires when p95 > 2500ms for 15m (warn) and > 5000ms for 10m (page), scoped to the insights query service.

3) Runbook stub: Adds a runbook page with investigation steps and links to relevant services/datasets (warehouse partitions, ingestion pipeline, billing hook job).

Metric assumptions (coordinated with Telemetry/Analytics): This PR uses the metric names already in-flight on redwood (see ENG-3824/ENG-3817): usage_insights_aggregation_lag_seconds{dataset,region,deployment_mode}; usage_insights_late_event_ratio{region,streaming}; usage_insights_missing_route_tag_rate{region}; usage_insights_reconciliation_drift_pct{workspace_id}; usage_insights_query_request_duration_ms_bucket{route,status}; usage_insights_query_errors_total{route,status}. If any naming changes land before beta, we can follow up with a patch release of observability-pack.

Noise/guardrails: The drift alert includes a minimum spend floor and a 2h lookback to avoid paging on transient rounding/timing skew (e.g., hourly partitions catching up). The lag alert pages only on sustained lag > 60m.

Testing/validation: Imported dashboard into staging Grafana; validated queries on canary metrics in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Used replayed lag/drift fixtures from staging to verify alert routing and labels.

Rollout notes: This is safe to ship ahead of beta. SREs should import the dashboard to the internal "Console & Tooling" folder and ensure Alertmanager routes page severity=page to #eng-sre, and warn severity=warn to #eng-platform.

Screenshots: (not included in PR; Grafana JSON is the source of truth).

title: Add Shadow Traffic quality delta dashboards + scoring lag panels (cardinality-safe)
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a first-cut set of Grafana dashboards to visualize Shadow Traffic quality deltas (control vs shadow variants) and scoring pipeline lag/health. The dashboards are designed for Hosted/Dedicated/Private and intentionally avoid high-cardinality breakdowns (no prompt_id, request_fingerprint, raw route_id, customer_id).

This is intended to support beta oncall readiness and help triage the two main failure classes seen during dogfood/beta:
1) quality deltas missing/stale due to scoring lag or ingestion lag
2) metrics backend pressure due to accidental high-cardinality dimensions

## What’s included
- New "Shadow Traffic / Quality Deltas" dashboard:
  - Δ quality score (shadow - control) over time with confidence bands (when emitted)
  - refusal rate delta, format adherence delta
  - sample size panels (n_control, n_shadow) + effective sample rate
  - breakdowns limited to:
    - deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private)
    - model_family (e.g., llama3, qwen2, mistral)
    - route_group (sanitized bucket: "core_chat", "embeddings", "tools", etc.)
    - rubric_version (pinned)
    - experiment_id (capped / top-k only)

- New "Shadow Traffic / Scoring Lag & Worker Health" dashboard:
  - scoring queue lag (p50/p95/p99) + oldest message age
  - worker throughput, retry rate, DLQ count
  - ingestion lag for shadow_pair events
  - circuit breaker open rate + dropped shadow attempts (fail-open)

- Alert rule proposals (NOT enabled by default):
  - scoring pipeline lag SLO breach (ticket -> page escalation guidance in annotations)
  - missing scoring signal (no scored pairs for X minutes while shadow attempts > 0)
  - quality regression advisory alert (requires minimum sample size + CI)

## Cardinality / safety notes
This PR follows the cardinality constraints discussed in SUP-28510 and the Shadow Metrics ADR:
- No dashboards query by prompt_id/request_fingerprint.
- experiment_id panels use a top-k query and default to aggregate view.
- route_id is replaced with route_group generated upstream (low-cardinality mapping maintained by telemetry).
- model_id is avoided; we use model_family + (optional) variant_role (control|shadow).

## Files changed (high level)
- dashboards/shadow_traffic_quality_deltas.jsonnet
- dashboards/shadow_scoring_lag_and_health.jsonnet
- dashboards/_library/shadow_traffic_panels.libsonnet
- alerts/shadow_scoring_lag_rules.yml (proposed)
- alerts/shadow_quality_regression_rules.yml (proposed)
- dashboards/README.md (usage + label guidance)

## Example panel queries (illustrative)
- Quality delta (aggregate):
  sum(rate(redwood_shadow_quality_score_sum{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m])) / sum(rate(redwood_shadow_quality_score_count{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m]))
  -
  sum(rate(redwood_control_quality_score_sum{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m])) / sum(rate(redwood_control_quality_score_count{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m]))

- Scoring lag (p95):
  histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, deployment_mode) (rate(redwood_shadow_scoring_lag_seconds_bucket{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode"}[5m])))

## Rollout plan
- Merge to observability-pack, dashboards deployed to internal Grafana first (dogfood tenants)
- Enable for Dedicated beta tenants once telemetry labels are confirmed stable (route_group + model_family present)
- Alert rules remain "proposed" until SRE signs off on thresholds and paging policy

## Screenshots
Not included in PR description (dashboards are generated in CI and reviewed via preview JSON in artifacts). See CI artifact: grafana-dashboards-preview.zip

## Checklist
- [x] Panels avoid high-cardinality dimensions
- [x] Default views are aggregate-first; breakdowns are opt-in
- [x] Added dashboard annotations linking to runbook sections (shadow scoring lag / missing data)
- [x] CI renders jsonnet and validates Grafana schema

## Notes / follow-ups
- Follow-up: add a small "label sanitizer" doc with examples of safe/unsafe breakdowns (Logan/Nadia request)
- Follow-up: once confidence interval emission is standardized, switch CI panel to render CI bands instead of placeholder series

Related:
- ENG-5409 (alerting quality regression detection)
- ENG-5410 (cardinality guardrails for shadow metrics)
- SUP-28477 (missing quality dashboard data for Dedicated tenant)
- SUP-28510 (cardinality alerts after enabling shadow traffic)

title: Add incident taxonomy dashboards + alerting (type/owner breakdowns + playbook links)
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds the initial Incident Taxonomy (v1) dashboards + a small set of alert rules, per the reliability program initiative. The goal is to make taxonomy classification measurable and to enable reporting/triage by incident_type + owner_team without exploding metric label cardinality.

Key deliverables in this PR:
- Grafana dashboard: **Incident Taxonomy (v1)**
  - incident volume and impact proxy panels (by tier, severity)
  - breakdowns by **incident_type** and **owner_team**
  - classification compliance (% unknown/unclassified)
  - MTTA/MTTR approximations using incident event timestamps
  - drilldowns from type -> owner -> recent incidents (table)
  - playbook links based on incident_type (panel links + table column)
- Prometheus rules:
  - recording rules to normalize/aggregate incident event counters into a stable set of labels
  - alert rules for (1) unclassified rate regression (2) spike of incidents by type/owner (3) sustained active incidents for Dedicated/Private

## Context / Motivation
As part of the Reliability Program (“Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”), incident_type/subtype and owner are now required fields in incident tooling. We need dashboards/alerts that:
- show whether classification is being used correctly (and where it’s not)
- create a feedback loop for top recurring incident categories
- allow on-call to quickly jump from “what’s happening” -> “who owns it” -> “what runbook should I follow”

This PR targets the spec in Confluence (Incident taxonomy dashboards spec) and ties into Linear ENG-4828.

## Design notes (cardinality + data sources)
Data sources used:
- `incident_events_total{event=...}` emitted from incident-bot pipeline / annotations ingestion
- existing `request_errors_total` and `request_latency_seconds` are NOT relabeled here; the dashboard focuses on incidents as the unit of analysis

Cardinality constraints:
- `incident_type` is treated as a strict enum (v1 allowlist)
- `owner_team` is constrained to a small allowlist of on-call teams (e.g., SRE, Fleet, Routing, Runtime, Observability, SecurityOps, PrivateDeploy)
- `incident_subtype` is intentionally not used as a Prometheus label in this PR (shown only in the incident table sourced from annotations/events)
- `unknown` is a first-class bucket to avoid missing data (explicitly tracked)

## What changed
### Dashboards
- New dashboard: `dashboards/incident-taxonomy-v1.jsonnet`
  - Variables: `tier`, `severity`, `owner_team`, `incident_type`, `env`, `region`
  - Panels:

    - “Incidents created by owner_team”

    - `ri:incidents_unclassified:count{tier,severity}`

  - `SustainedActiveIncidentsDedicatedPrivate` (page): active incidents >= 2 for 30m for Dedicated/Private

(Attached in PR UI)

- Grafana dashboard: **Incident Taxonomy (v1)**

  - MTTA/MTTR approximations using incident event timestamps
  - drilldowns from type -> owner -> recent incidents (table)
  - playbook links based on incident_type (panel links + table column)
- Prometheus rules:
  - recording rules to normalize/aggregate incident event counters into a stable set of labels
  - alert rules for (1) unclassified rate regression (2) spike of incidents by type/owner (3) sustained active incidents for Dedicated/Private

## Context / Motivation
As part of the Reliability Program (“Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”), incident_type/subtype and owner are now required fields in incident tooling. We need dashboards/alerts that:

title: perf-canary: add warning-level alert rules (dampening + min sample size) routed to #eng-runtime
preview:
## Summary
Adds the initial set of warning-level perf-canary alerts (no paging) to detect per-model serving performance regressions early, with explicit guardrails to reduce noise during ramp.

This PR wires:
- Warning alerts for p95 latency regression and tokens/sec drops
- Dampening (sustained windows) + min-sample conditions
- Alertmanager routing to Slack #eng-runtime
- Runbook + dashboard links in annotations

This is intended to be enabled first in us-east with a limited model list (per ENG-4880 rollout plan). Paging rules are tracked separately (pr-19183).

## Motivation / context
During early perf-canary rollout we saw false positives driven by low volume, baseline drift, and transient telemetry delays. The warning rules in this PR aim to be:
- Low-noise: only fire when there’s enough data
- Actionable: include baseline vs observed deltas + direct links
- Safe: warning-only channel routing while we calibrate

## Alerting approach (v0)
### What we alert on
1) **p95 end-to-end latency regression** (primary): observed p95 vs baseline p95
2) **tokens/sec drop** (secondary): observed tokens/sec vs baseline tokens/sec

### Key guardrails
- **Minimum sample size**: require >= N completed canary requests in the evaluation window
- **Dampening**: require condition to hold for a continuous duration before firing
- **Cardinality limits**: alert labels only include model + region + tier + runtime_build_id (no prompt-shape label)

### Windows / thresholds (v0 defaults)
- p95 latency regression: >= +12% for 15m (warning)
- tokens/sec drop: <= -10% for 15m (warning)
- min samples: >= 200 requests in 15m per (model, region, tier)

These defaults are intentionally conservative; per-model overrides will be managed via the baseline/threshold config in the perf-canary service and/or follow-up PR if we need rule-level overrides.

## Implementation details
- Adds alerting rules under `alerts/perf_canary/warnings.yaml`
- Adds an Alertmanager route/receiver for `slack_eng_runtime` with a grouping key on (alertname, model, region, tier)
- Adds templates used to render compact Slack messages including baseline/observed/threshold and deep links

### Example rule snippet
The core pattern is:

- Ensure sufficient volume:
  `sum(increase(perf_canary_requests_total{status="ok"}[15m])) by (model, region, tier) >= 200`

- Compare observed p95 to baseline p95 (both expressed in ms):
  `histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(perf_canary_e2e_latency_ms_bucket[5m])) by (le, model, region, tier)) / on(model, region, tier) group_left perf_canary_baseline_p95_latency_ms`

- Fire when ratio exceeds threshold for long enough:
  `> 1.12 for: 15m`

Annotations include:
- `runbook_url`: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Runbook%3A+Perf+Regression+Canary+Alert
- `dashboard_url`: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/perf-canary/perf-canary?var-model={{ $labels.model }}&var-region={{ $labels.region }}

## Testing
- `promtool check rules` in CI
- `promtool test rules` added/updated for:
  - happy path: regression sustained with min samples -> fires
  - low-volume path: regression but samples < min -> does not fire
  - transient spike: regression for < for-window -> does not fire
- Alertmanager config validation

## Rollout plan
1) Merge rules disabled by default (gated by `perf_canary_alerts_enabled` config in the alerting pipeline)
2) Enable in us-east for 2–3 models with only warning routing
3) Tune thresholds/min-samples if needed (ENG-4882)
4) Add paging rules separately once warning noise is acceptable (pr-19183)

## Risks / mitigations
- Risk: telemetry ingestion delay causes late datapoints and apparent regressions
  - Mitigation: long `for:` window + min samples; dashboard links include ingestion-lag panel (from pr-19174)
- Risk: baseline drift causes slow burn alerts
  - Mitigation: warning-only; follow-up ADR work for baseline update policy

## Screenshots
N/A (rule-based); see CI output for promtool rule tests.

## Checklist
- [x] Warning alerts only (no paging)
- [x] Min sample size guardrails
- [x] Dampening windows
- [x] Slack routing to #eng-runtime
- [x] Runbook + dashboard links
- [x] promtool validation + tests

Related:
- Dashboards: pr-19174
- Paging rules: pr-19183
- Perf-canary payload formatting: perf-canary pr-19244

title: Perf baseline dashboards + alert rules for representative suite (TTFT/TPOT/throughput/variance) with manifest links
preview: Context: As part of the Q1'26 perf baseline + representative workload suite, we need a standard set of dashboards and alerting that (a) make baseline runs explorable, (b) make regressions actionable, and (c) are attributable via a first-class link from the panel to the run manifest + trace artifacts. This PR adds a versioned dashboard bundle and alert rules aimed at baseline runs + perf-canary outputs. What’s included: 1) Grafana dashboards (folder: "Perf Baselines") with panels for TTFT/TPOT percentiles, throughput, saturation proxies, and variance/noise views. 2) Alert rules for baseline regressions (non-paging by default) and canary gating signals (paging for release-branch gating only) with routing to the right channels/oncall. 3) Standard run label/templating conventions aligned with ADR-0172 (model, model_family, workload_id, region, sku, runtime_git_sha, scheduler_profile, kernel_profile, cache_mode, run_id). 4) Panel links: dashboard → run manifest (JSON) → trace bundle (if present). Links are built from run_id + artifact_base_url labels so we don’t have to hardcode per-environment paths. 5) A small metrics dictionary excerpt in-repo to keep dashboards stable while the full dictionary draft is iterated in Drive. Dashboards: - "Perf Baseline — Overview": top-line TTFT/TPOT p50/p95/p99 and throughput for the selected run cohort; quick filters for region/SKU/model/workload/cache_mode; includes a "cold vs warm" compare row. - "Perf Baseline — Latency detail": TTFT and TPOT with per-stage breakdown (client boundary vs server boundary where available), plus queue_wait_ms and batch_assembly_ms panels when the runtime emits them. - "Perf Baseline — Throughput & saturation": tokens/s, req/s (embeddings), GPU utilization (if present), batch size distribution, and a "saturation heuristics" row (queue depth, shed counters, backpressure). - "Perf Baseline — Variance": run-to-run variability panels (median absolute deviation + coefficient of variation) and a "noise budget" view to help separate regressions from fleet variability; includes warmup window visualization based on run_phase label. - "Perf Baseline — Regression compare": diff view against a selected baseline anchor (baseline_run_id) with percent deltas for p95/p99 TTFT/TPOT and throughput; flags when deltas exceed configured thresholds. Alerts: - perf_baseline_regression_warning: triggers when p99 TTFT or p99 TPOT regresses > 5% vs baseline anchor for a given (model, workload, region, sku, cache_mode) cohort, sustained over N=5 samples (configurable). Routes to #eng-runtime-perf-triage and tags the cohort in the alert annotations. - perf_baseline_variance_spike: triggers when variance (CV) exceeds threshold, indicating fleet instability; routes to #eng-sre-benchmark-fleet (non-paging). - perf_canary_gating_failure: only enabled for release branches (via label enable_gating=true); pages runtime oncall if p99 TTFT/TPOT regresses beyond gating thresholds for required cohorts. Notes on measurement boundaries: Dashboards assume TTFT/TPOT are server-side timings emitted by the benchmark harness/runtime instrumentation (not client wall-clock). We still show "first-token-on-wire" when present, but it’s not used for gating alerts. Manifest/trace linkouts: Panel links use a templated URL: ${artifact_base_url}/runs/${run_id}/manifest.json and ${artifact_base_url}/runs/${run_id}/traces/ (the trace link is conditional; shows as "Trace artifacts" with a warning when the label trace_available=false). Rollout plan: Merge behind folder-level RBAC (Engineering only). Alerts ship in disabled-by-default state for 48h dry run in staging; then enable warning alerts in prod; gating/paging remains controlled via enable_gating label and release branch policy. Testing: Validated dashboard JSON imports cleanly on grafana-staging and prod sandbox. Verified alert expressions against 2 historical baseline runs (chat_streaming and long_prefill) and confirmed annotation links resolve to manifest artifacts from benchmark-lab uploads. Screenshots: Attached in PR comments (overview + variance panel examples).

title: Add hot-route protection dashboards + alert rules (enterprise tier + shed reasons)
preview: Context: As part of Hot-route Capacity Protection (ENG-4926), SRE needs a consistent set of dashboards + alert rules for protected routes (/v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings, /v1/rerank) that make it easy to answer: (1) are enterprise routes staying within SLO under fleet pressure, (2) are we shedding/throttling the right tier first, and (3) what is the reason breakdown (quota vs overload vs admission control) when 429s occur. This PR adds Grafana dashboard definitions (jsonnet) and Prometheus alert rules for the new metrics emitted by gateway/routing/runtime (see redwood PRs for instrumentation and 429 reason codes). Scope: dashboards (per-route/per-tier latency + error + shed), annotations for protection activation events, and paging rules focused on enterprise degradation (not expected best-effort shedding). What’s included: (A) Dashboards: 1) Hot Route Protection – Overview: request volume by route group + tier, 429 rate by reason, shed/admission-control rate, queue depth / scheduler lag signals, and 5xx. 2) Hot Route Protection – Enterprise SLO: p50/p95/p99 latency by protected route group for enterprise tier, availability (2xx/3xx) and error budget burn panels, with burn windows (5m/1h/6h). 3) Hot Route Protection – Debug: top tenants (sampled/low-cardinality) by route group, saturation indicators, and correlation panels for policy-eval latency + routing decision outcomes. (B) Alerts (Prometheus rules): 1) Page: enterprise protected route latency burn > 2x for 15m OR fast burn > 10x for 5m (gated to enterprise tier + protected route groups). 2) Page: enterprise protected route 5xx rate > 1% for 10m (excluding client cancellations). 3) Ticket: sustained overload protection activation (shed_rate_overload_reason > threshold for 30m) when enterprise SLO remains healthy (expected during pressure) to drive capacity follow-up without paging storms. 4) Ticket: missing/flatlined metrics for new overload reason series (helps catch ingestion regressions and the cardinality hotfix class of issues). Notes/guardrails: All queries follow the overload metrics cardinality guidance: no tenant_id in primary panels; we use route_group, tier, region, reason_code only. The “Debug” dashboard includes an opt-in variable for sampled tenant (uses pre-aggregated topK series). Alert rules intentionally avoid paging on best-effort 429 spikes alone; we page only when enterprise SLO signals degrade. Rollout: dashboards are safe to merge before full multi-region enablement; panels will show ‘No data’ until metrics are present. Alert rules are shipped disabled by default via env toggle in the pack; enablement to follow GA checklist (eng-releases thread + canary results). Links: Linear ENG-4926 (dashboards/alerts), ENG-4927 (incident-bot notifications). Related: slo-toolkit PR for standard burn alert helper functions, redwood PR adding overload reason codes to 429s, and runtime PR exporting queue depth + scheduler lag.

(B) Alerts (Prometheus rules): 1) Page: enterprise protected route latency burn > 2x for 15m OR fast burn > 10x for 5m (gated to enterprise tier + protected route groups). 2) Page: enterprise protected route 5xx rate > 1% for 10m (excluding client cancellations). 3) Ticket: sustained overload protection activation (shed_rate_overload_reason > threshold for 30m) when enterprise SLO remains healthy (expected during pressure) to drive capacity follow-up without paging storms. 4) Ticket: missing/flatlined metrics for new overload reason series (helps catch ingestion regressions and the cardinality hotfix class of issues). Notes/guardrails: All queries follow the overload metrics cardinality guidance: no tenant_id in primary panels; we use route_group, tier, region, reason_code only. The “Debug” dashboard includes an opt-in variable for sampled tenant (uses pre-aggregated topK series). Alert rules intentionally avoid paging on best-effort 429 spikes alone; we page only when enterprise SLO signals degrade. Rollout: dashboards are safe to merge before full multi-region enablement; panels will show ‘No data’ until metrics are present. Alert rules are shipped disabled by default via env toggle in the pack; enablement to follow GA checklist (eng-releases thread + canary results). Links: Linear ENG-4926 (dashboards/alerts), ENG-4927 (incident-bot notifications). Related: slo-toolkit PR for standard burn alert helper functions, redwood PR adding overload reason codes to 429s, and runtime PR exporting queue depth + scheduler lag.

title: Add activation funnel dashboard panels + suggested alerts for regressions
preview:
Context: This is part of the Lifecycle Growth Experiments sprint (Hosted API activation + early retention). We need a standardized, self-serve dashboard that answers: (1) where do new signups drop in the funnel, (2) which experiment cohorts/variants moved the needle, and (3) how do we catch regressions quickly after email/nudge/retargeting launches.

What’s included in this PR: Adds a set of activation funnel panels to the existing Growth/PLG dashboard pack and wires in suggested (disabled-by-default) alert rules. The dashboard focuses on signup -> API key created -> first successful request -> first 100k tokens within 7d, plus TTFT distributions.

Dashboard updates: - New row: “Activation funnel (new signups)” with conversion panels per step (absolute counts + rates), and a time series of overall activation_rate_7d (first_request within 7d of signup). - Breakdown panels: conversion by region, SDK (ts/python/rest), and experiment cohort (feature flag holdout vs treatment). - Variant drilldowns: email_variant_id, nudge_id, and utm_campaign (for retargeting) as optional breakdown dimensions (these are behind dashboard variables to avoid exploding cardinality by default). - Latency: p50/p95 TTFT for first successful request (filtered to first_request events only) with a note about sampling and outlier handling. - Data quality: panel that tracks event ingestion gaps (% missing region, % missing sdk) and a warning annotation if the dedupe-fix version hasn’t fully rolled out.

Suggested alerts (added as templates, not auto-enabled): - Activation regression: activation_rate_7d drops >15% vs 28d baseline for 2h (with minimum N guardrail). - Funnel step regression: api_key_created_rate and first_request_rate drops separately, to help localize which step is broken. - TTFT regression: p95 TTFT for first request increases >25% vs baseline (suppressed if volume is low).

Notes/assumptions: - Queries expect the standardized event taxonomy from ENG-10492 (signup/api_key_created/first_request/first_100k_tokens) and will be most accurate once the duplicate-event emission fix is fully deployed (see redwood hotfix PR referenced in ENG-10492 notes). - All breakdowns default to low-cardinality dimensions; high-cardinality drilldowns are opt-in via variables.

Testing: - jsonnet render + schema validation via `make dashboards` - alert rule linting via `make alerts-lint` - manual spot-check in Grafana: panels load under 2s for last 7d; breakdowns under 5s with variables enabled.

Rollout: No runtime impact. Once merged, dashboards ship with the next observability-pack sync. Alert templates require explicit enablement per env (stage/prod) and per oncall ownership.

Screenshots: included in the PR conversation (Grafana panel previews + variable dropdowns).

title: Add rollback dashboard panels + annotations for Optimization Config apply/promote/rollback events
preview: Summary: This PR updates the "Optimization Rollout Health" Grafana dashboard in observability-pack to make safe apply + rollback operations visible and diagnosable during canaries and incident response. It adds (1) dashboard annotations for Optimization Config lifecycle events (apply, promote, rollback, gate pause/fail) and (2) panels that slice the core health signals by optimization_config_version to quickly confirm whether the rollout is causing elevated error/latency or saturation. Motivation: During the gameday drill and a recent customer escalation (SUP-18421) we spent too long correlating p95 regressions with the exact config version + rollback timestamp because charts weren’t consistently annotated and most panels lacked config_version breakdown. This is a prerequisite for GA readiness (PM-904) and supports the auditability story for enterprise (SUP-18544 follow-up: 'prove rollback happened at X time and the metrics recovered'). What’s included: - Annotations: Adds an annotation query that pulls Optimization Config events from the control plane event stream (exported into Loki) and renders vertical markers for apply/promote/rollback, scoped by tenant/model/region. Events are labeled with config_name, config_version, actor, and rollout_id. - Panels (new): (a) Error rate (5xx) by config_version, (b) p95/p99 latency by config_version, (c) Throughput + queue depth, (d) GPU utilization + KV cache hit rate, all with templated variables tenant/model/region and a new variable optimization_config_version. - Panels (updated): Existing "Overall health" panels now include legend formatting to surface config_version when present and fall back gracefully for older workers that don’t emit the tag yet. - Templating: Adds variables for rollout_id and config_version with sensible defaults; when unset, panels aggregate across versions as before. - Documentation: Updates dashboard README to document required metric tags (tenant, model, region, optimization_config_version) and the minimum versions of runtime/control-plane exporters required for annotations to populate. Compatibility/rollout notes: - Metrics: Panels use the new tag name "optimization_config_version" (per the metrics spec agreed with platform/runtime). If a cluster is still on older exporters, those series simply won’t appear in the by-version panels; the aggregate panels remain usable. - Logs/annotations: Annotation query relies on control-plane events being shipped to Loki under stream {app="control-plane", component="opt-config"}. Private deployments can wire the same annotation by shipping audit/event logs, but it’s optional; doc notes included. Testing: - Verified dashboards render locally in grafana-dev with the sample Prometheus/Loki datasets. - Ran jsonnet compilation + grafana-lint in CI. - Smoke-checked templating and annotation query performance (kept to 24h default with user-controlled range). Screenshots: Attached in the PR UI: 1) Annotated latency chart showing apply->promote->rollback markers, 2) Error rate panel split by optimization_config_version, 3) Variable dropdowns for config_version/rollout_id. Follow-ups (not in scope): - Add a "gate decision" panel that displays the evaluated gate status over time (pending signal from slo-toolkit integration). - Add a compact "Rollback summary" row for on-call (MTTD/MTTR inputs) once incident-bot emits incident_id tags.

title: perf-canary dashboards: add per-model p95 latency + tokens/sec panels (with breakdowns + runbook links)
preview:
## Summary
Adds the initial Grafana dashboard panels for the production performance canary. The goal is fast triage for per-model regressions (p95 latency and tokens/sec) with enough breakdowns to distinguish kernel/runtime execution regressions from scheduler/queueing and infra issues.

This PR is dashboards-only (no alert rules). Alerts are tracked separately so we can iterate on panel queries/labels before we encode anything into paging logic.

## What’s included
- New dashboard: **Perf Canary / Per-model Performance (Prod)**
  - Topline panels per selected model:
    - End-to-end latency p50/p95/p99 (histogram quantiles)
    - Runtime-only latency p50/p95/p99 (when available)
    - Tokens/sec (overall) + tokens/sec (decode-only) where emitted
  - Breakdown panels:
    - Prefill latency vs decode latency (stacked / side-by-side)
    - Queueing time (scheduler) and queueing fraction of end-to-end
    - Batch size distribution (p50/p95 + histogram heatmap)
    - KV cache hit rate (if the metric is present for the target model/runtime)
  - Correlation / attribution helpers:
    - Runtime build tag and kernel selection tag (as table + annotation)
    - GPU hardware SKU selector and region selector (to isolate fleet changes)
  - Drilldowns:
    - Explore link to raw series (preserves template vars)
    - Trace search link (where trace_id exemplars exist) for recent spikes

## Template variables (kept intentionally small)
- `model` (required)
- `region` (defaults to `us-east`, multi-select enabled)
- `tier` (hosted|dedicated|private, defaults to `hosted`)
- `hardware_sku` (optional; hidden by default to avoid casual cardinality blowups)
- `runtime_build` (optional; used primarily during rollouts)

Note: we do **not** template on `prompt_shape` / `bucket` even though canary emits shape buckets. Those panels are included as a separate collapsed row (opt-in) because we saw the label surface expand quickly during early experiments.

## Metric/query notes
- Uses the canonical perf-canary metric names agreed in ENG-4873:
  - `perf_canary_request_latency_seconds_bucket` (e2e)
  - `perf_canary_runtime_latency_seconds_bucket` (runtime-only)
  - `perf_canary_tokens_per_second` (gauge)
  - `perf_canary_queueing_seconds_bucket` (scheduler)
  - `perf_canary_batch_size` (histogram)
  - `perf_canary_kv_cache_hit_ratio` (gauge)
- Histogram quantiles are computed with `histogram_quantile()` over `rate(<bucket>[5m])`.
- Tokens/sec uses `avg_over_time(...[5m])` with a 1m min step to reduce graph jitter.
- All panels include `model`, `region`, and `tier` in the legend; other dimensions are shown only when explicitly filtered.

## Runbook links
Each top-level row includes a link to the on-call runbook for canary regressions:
- Confluence: `eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert`
- The link is parameterized via dashboard variables (model/region/tier) so the runbook opens with context in the URL.

## Screenshots
- Attached in the PR UI (see: “Per-model overview row”, “Breakdown row”, “Annotations + drilldowns”).

## Rollout plan
- Land in observability-pack, then enable in `prod` Grafana folder via the normal dashboard sync job.
- Runtime/SRE will sanity check the panels during Phase 1 rollout (us-east, 2–3 models).

## Testing
- `make dashboards-lint`
- `make dashboards-render` (jsonnet render)
- Verified in staging Grafana against canary metrics (us-east) for: `llama-3.1-70b-instruct` and `qwen2.5-32b-instruct`.

## Follow-ups
- Add annotation feed for control-plane canary model list changes (so changes in coverage are obvious on the graphs).
- Add a small “alert preview” row (not rules) showing computed deviation vs baseline once baseline store is stable.

Refs: ENG-4877, ENG-4873

title: Reduce cardinality for incident_type metric label (allowlist + sampling + unknown buckets)
preview: Context: PR #1459 introduced incident taxonomy dashboards and alert rules. During rollout review, Observability flagged that naively labeling high-volume request/edge metrics with incident_type can explode series cardinality (especially when incident-bot classification is missing/late, or when teams attempt to encode subtypes/notes). This follow-up tightens the metric labeling contract and adjusts recording rules to keep the dashboards useful without putting Prometheus/Mimir under stress. What changed: 1) Added an explicit allowlist for incident_type values used in metrics (top-level types only, no freeform). 2) Introduced two stable buckets for missing/invalid classification: incident_type="unknown" (not set) and incident_type="unclassified" (set but not in allowlist / failed validation). 3) Moved incident_type labeling off the highest-cardinality per-route/per-model series and onto lower-cardinality rollups used by the dashboards (recording rules). 4) Added a conservative sampling strategy for the remaining labeled raw series (only for error events and only for a limited set of metrics), with clear comments and defaults. 5) Updated dashboard queries and alert annotations to treat unknown/unclassified explicitly and to keep them visible (we want to drive down unknowns, not hide them). Guidance / policy baked into this repo: - Metrics must only use the top-level incident_type enum (no subtype) and must not include user/customer identifiers. - If classification is missing at emission time, emit incident_type="unknown". If a value is present but not in allowlist, normalize to incident_type="unclassified" and emit a counter for visibility. - Dashboards should break out unknown/unclassified as first-class categories so SRE can track adoption and decision-tree gaps. Rollout notes: This should be safe to deploy independently of incident-bot changes. It assumes the incident_type field exists in log/metric enrichment but tolerates it being absent. After merge, please re-run the cardinality check job in staging and confirm series counts stay within the thresholds in docs/metrics/cardinality.md. Related: ENG-4826 (metric label guardrails), ENG-4828 (dashboard build), and the earlier dashboard PR #1459.
