title: Add dashboard panels for hardware profile selection + tail latency regressions
preview:
Context: As we roll out the hardware tuning profile pack (H100/L40S first; B200 gated), oncall and perf owners need to answer two questions quickly: (1) which profile was actually selected (and why), and (2) did enabling the profile cause a tail-latency regression in a specific pool/region/model/workload slice. This PR updates our Grafana dashboards in observability-pack to add segmentation by `hardware_profile` (selected profile name + version) and `hardware_profile_source` (fleet default vs per-pool vs per-deployment override), and adds focused panels for tail behavior (p95/p99) with rollout-friendly breakdowns.

What’s included (high level): Adds a new dashboard row “Hardware profile selection” to the runtime/inference overview dashboard, showing selected profile distribution, profile source distribution, and a table of “top offenders” (pool/region/model) where p95/p99 is above thresholds while profile is enabled. Adds a dedicated “Rollout: Hardware Profiles” dashboard intended for staged rollouts with side-by-side comparison panels (profile-enabled vs profile-disabled) and a ‘regression highlight’ view for p95 TTFT and end-to-end latency. Updates existing tail-latency panels to include profile labels in legends and to prefer quantile histograms (vs gauge-like summaries) to reduce false confidence during canaries.

Dependencies/assumptions: This PR assumes the runtime exports the following labels on request latency and throughput metrics (added in runtime PRs referenced from the hardware profiles project): `hardware_profile`, `hardware_profile_version`, `hardware_profile_source` and that they are present on `redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket` (and the corresponding `*_count`/`*_sum`) and `redwood_inference_tokens_generated_total`. If a pool is running an older runtime (no labels), panels will still render but will show `hardware_profile="unknown"` via label_replace fallback. The queries are written to degrade gracefully.

Key dashboard changes:
- Runtime / Inference Overview:
  - New row: “Hardware profile selection”
    - Panel: Selected profile distribution (time series, stacked) by `hardware_profile`.
    - Panel: Override source distribution (time series, stacked) by `hardware_profile_source`.
    - Panel: “Top p99 latency (profile-enabled)” table filtered to `hardware_profile_source!=none` with columns (pool, region, model, profile, p99, req/s).
  - Existing tail latency panels updated:
    - Legends now include `{hardware_profile}/{hardware_profile_source}`.
    - Added a dashboard variable `profile` (regex/all) and `profile_source` for quick filtering during incidents.

- New dashboard: “Rollout: Hardware Profiles (SRE/Perf)”
  - Variables: pool, region, model, gpu_sku, profile, profile_version, profile_source.
  - Panels:
    - p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency (histogram_quantile) split by profile.
    - TTFT (time-to-first-token) p95/p99 split by profile.
    - Tokens/sec (rate of tokens) split by profile.
    - Queue wait p95 (if present) split by profile.
    - “Regression highlight” stat: ratio of p95 (profile-enabled / profile-disabled) over a selectable window.
  - Notes panel: operational guidance + links to the rollout runbook and perf-canary gates.

PromQL notes (representative snippets):
- End-to-end p95 latency by profile:
  - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, hardware_profile, hardware_profile_source) (rate(redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket{route=~"$route",pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval])))
- TTFT p99 by profile:
  - histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le, hardware_profile) (rate(redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket{pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval])))
- Tokens/sec by profile:
  - sum by (hardware_profile) (rate(redwood_inference_tokens_generated_total{pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval]))

Operational intent: During canary rollouts, SREs should filter to the canary pool + region and compare ‘profile-enabled’ vs ‘unknown/none’. The regression panels are meant to provide a fast signal, not a guarantee. We’re intentionally using p95/p99 and not just p50 because the profile pack can trade throughput for tail stability (or vice versa), and tail is what tends to page.

Out of scope: This PR does not change alert rules (Prometheus alerts) directly; it only adds/updates dashboards and documentation notes. Alert threshold tuning remains tracked under ENG-4828 and in the perf-canary repo.

Testing: Rendered dashboards locally via jsonnet, validated queries against staging Prometheus (us-east, H100 pool) and confirmed panels don’t error when `hardware_profile` labels are missing. Also verified that label cardinality remains within acceptable bounds by keeping profile/version labels on a small set of dashboards rather than globally duplicating panels.

Links: Linear ENG-4828/4829. Related runtime metrics: redwood PR-22027 (profile selection metrics) and perf-canary PR-731 (gates). Support context: SUP-29410 (customer-reported latency spike correlated with config rollout).
text:
Context: As we roll out the hardware tuning profile pack (H100/L40S first; B200 gated), oncall and perf owners need to answer two questions quickly: (1) which profile was actually selected (and why), and (2) did enabling the profile cause a tail-latency regression in a specific pool/region/model/workload slice. This PR updates our Grafana dashboards in observability-pack to add segmentation by `hardware_profile` (selected profile name + version) and `hardware_profile_source` (fleet default vs per-pool vs per-deployment override), and adds focused panels for tail behavior (p95/p99) with rollout-friendly breakdowns.

What’s included (high level): Adds a new dashboard row “Hardware profile selection” to the runtime/inference overview dashboard, showing selected profile distribution, profile source distribution, and a table of “top offenders” (pool/region/model) where p95/p99 is above thresholds while profile is enabled. Adds a dedicated “Rollout: Hardware Profiles” dashboard intended for staged rollouts with side-by-side comparison panels (profile-enabled vs profile-disabled) and a ‘regression highlight’ view for p95 TTFT and end-to-end latency. Updates existing tail-latency panels to include profile labels in legends and to prefer quantile histograms (vs gauge-like summaries) to reduce false confidence during canaries.

Dependencies/assumptions: This PR assumes the runtime exports the following labels on request latency and throughput metrics (added in runtime PRs referenced from the hardware profiles project): `hardware_profile`, `hardware_profile_version`, `hardware_profile_source` and that they are present on `redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket` (and the corresponding `*_count`/`*_sum`) and `redwood_inference_tokens_generated_total`. If a pool is running an older runtime (no labels), panels will still render but will show `hardware_profile="unknown"` via label_replace fallback. The queries are written to degrade gracefully.

Key dashboard changes:
- Runtime / Inference Overview:
  - New row: “Hardware profile selection”
    - Panel: Selected profile distribution (time series, stacked) by `hardware_profile`.
    - Panel: Override source distribution (time series, stacked) by `hardware_profile_source`.
    - Panel: “Top p99 latency (profile-enabled)” table filtered to `hardware_profile_source!=none` with columns (pool, region, model, profile, p99, req/s).
  - Existing tail latency panels updated:
    - Legends now include `{hardware_profile}/{hardware_profile_source}`.
    - Added a dashboard variable `profile` (regex/all) and `profile_source` for quick filtering during incidents.

- New dashboard: “Rollout: Hardware Profiles (SRE/Perf)”
  - Variables: pool, region, model, gpu_sku, profile, profile_version, profile_source.
  - Panels:
    - p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency (histogram_quantile) split by profile.
    - TTFT (time-to-first-token) p95/p99 split by profile.
    - Tokens/sec (rate of tokens) split by profile.
    - Queue wait p95 (if present) split by profile.
    - “Regression highlight” stat: ratio of p95 (profile-enabled / profile-disabled) over a selectable window.
  - Notes panel: operational guidance + links to the rollout runbook and perf-canary gates.

PromQL notes (representative snippets):
- End-to-end p95 latency by profile:
  - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, hardware_profile, hardware_profile_source) (rate(redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket{route=~"$route",pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval])))
- TTFT p99 by profile:
  - histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le, hardware_profile) (rate(redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket{pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval])))
- Tokens/sec by profile:
  - sum by (hardware_profile) (rate(redwood_inference_tokens_generated_total{pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval]))

Operational intent: During canary rollouts, SREs should filter to the canary pool + region and compare ‘profile-enabled’ vs ‘unknown/none’. The regression panels are meant to provide a fast signal, not a guarantee. We’re intentionally using p95/p99 and not just p50 because the profile pack can trade throughput for tail stability (or vice versa), and tail is what tends to page.

Out of scope: This PR does not change alert rules (Prometheus alerts) directly; it only adds/updates dashboards and documentation notes. Alert threshold tuning remains tracked under ENG-4828 and in the perf-canary repo.

Testing: Rendered dashboards locally via jsonnet, validated queries against staging Prometheus (us-east, H100 pool) and confirmed panels don’t error when `hardware_profile` labels are missing. Also verified that label cardinality remains within acceptable bounds by keeping profile/version labels on a small set of dashboards rather than globally duplicating panels.

Links: Linear ENG-4828/4829. Related runtime metrics: redwood PR-22027 (profile selection metrics) and perf-canary PR-731 (gates). Support context: SUP-29410 (customer-reported latency spike correlated with config rollout).

title: Anti-flap logic (windows + hysteresis) for status-component health signals
preview: Motivation: Our current component health rollups (used to recommend public status changes and to populate status-page dashboards) are too twitchy. Short-lived spikes (e.g., 2–3 minutes of elevated 5xx or a brief p95 regression) can flip a component between Operational ↔ Degraded multiple times, which creates noisy on-call workflows and undermines customer trust in the status page. This PR implements anti-flap windows and hysteresis in the status-signal evaluator so we only transition component state when a condition persists long enough, and we require a healthier condition to recover than the one that caused the degradation. Summary of changes: Adds a small state machine around each status signal (errors + latency) with configurable (1) trigger window (min duration / min samples) and (2) recovery window (min duration / min samples) plus (3) hysteresis thresholds (degrade_threshold vs recover_threshold). Also adds per-component overrides and emits evaluator metrics so we can audit how often anti-flap prevented a transition. Behavior details: Degrade requires threshold breach for N consecutive evaluations (default 3 of 3 at 1m cadence) OR for a minimum time window (default 5m) depending on signal type; recover requires the metric to be back under a stricter recovery threshold for a longer window (default 10m). A note on semantics: this is intentionally separate from SLO burn alerts; we’re smoothing the customer-facing component health classification, not masking genuine outages. Rollout plan: guarded behind status_signals.anti_flap_enabled (default off). In dry-run mode we compute both old/new state and publish a diff metric (status_signals_state_diff_total{component_id,signal_type}) to validate no surprises. After 1 week of dry-run we’ll enable for Hosted API components in us-east + eu-west, then expand. Docs: Updated README in the pack and added inline docs for config knobs and default values. Testing: Adds unit tests for threshold crossing, jitter, and recovery hysteresis; adds a regression test that reproduces the us-east streaming ‘toggle’ pattern from early Nov. Related: Status page transparency upgrades project; aligns with internal standard for customer-facing status signals (anti-flap guidance + thresholds).

title: Link SLO burn alerts to status page update runbook + component taxonomy
preview: Context: As part of the status page transparency upgrades, on-call needs a fast, consistent path from a page to (a) the correct status-page component taxonomy and (b) the exact runbook steps for posting/updating component statuses. Today, most of our burn-rate alerts include a generic runbook link (or no runbook at all), and nothing points to the canonical taxonomy/mapping guidance. This PR updates alert annotations across component-level SLO burn alerts in this repo to include: (1) a status page update runbook link, and (2) a status page component taxonomy link. It also standardizes annotation keys so incident-bot and human responders can reliably surface the right links in Slack. Scope: alert rule annotations + lint/test updates only; no threshold or routing changes. Notes: we intentionally keep the links to high-level canonical docs rather than incident-specific pages, and we avoid language that implies an SLA/guarantee. Rollout: safe to merge; annotations-only; validated in staging alertmanager and via jsonnet render output. Follow-ups: once merged, we can have incident-bot surface these fields automatically in the intake + reminders (tracked elsewhere).

title: Add overload shedding dashboards + initial alert rules (breaker opens, shed rate)
preview: Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding reliability program, we need first-class visibility into (a) when we are shedding traffic vs failing implicitly, and (b) when breakers are opening/half-opening/closing across regions/models/tiers. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and initial Prometheus alert rules for overload protection signals. Scope: (1) Dashboards: Add "Hosted API • Overload Protection" dashboard group with panels for shed rate, breaker open rate, admission decisions, runtime saturation proxies (queue depth / token saturation / memory pressure proxy), and top shed reasons. Includes recommended drilldowns by region and route family, and a safe set of labels (bounded cardinality). (2) Alerts: Add first-pass paging + ticket alerts for (i) sustained breaker-open rate, (ii) elevated shed rate for paid tiers, (iii) Retry-After header missing on overload responses (via gateway metric), and (iv) mismatch between shed and runtime saturation (possible false positive / bad thresholds). Each alert includes runbook links and suggested next actions. (3) Runbook links: Links to oncall runbook and runtime mitigation guide; includes incident-bot channel references. Notes/guardrails: We intentionally avoid tenant_id and request_id as dimensions. Model is aggregated to a small allowlist via label mapping in the gateway metrics exporter. Dashboards default to last 1h and show p95/p99 lat overlay to correlate shedding with tail latency improvements. Follow-ups: (a) tighten alert thresholds after canary (false positives), (b) add per-tier SLO burn overlays once SLO toolkit classification is rolled out everywhere, (c) add a "top fallback attempts" panel after routing PR-18458 is fully deployed. Screenshots: See internal preview link in the Grafana folder (can be rendered in CI). Runbooks: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Hosted+Overload+Circuit+Breaker+Runbook and https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SERVING/Runtime+Overload+Mitigation. Testing: `make lint`, `make test`, `make dashboards-validate`, `make prom-rules-test`.

title: Add component-level SLO dashboards + standard links for status page components
preview: Context: As part of the Status Page Transparency Upgrades initiative, on-call needs a consistent, component-level view of latency + error impact that matches the public status component taxonomy (component_id x region x capability). Today dashboards are a mix of service names (gateway/scheduler/streamer), inconsistent region labels, and ad-hoc links in runbooks. This PR adds a standardized dashboard set per status component and a link registry so alerts/runbooks/status tooling can point to the same canonical URLs. What’s in this PR: (1) New Grafana dashboards for each public status component, split into latency + errors with consistent panels and templating. (2) Standard naming conventions for dashboards, folders, variables, and panel titles (to reduce “is this the right chart?” time during Sev1). (3) A generated link map (component_id/region/capability -> dashboard URL) committed under observability-pack so it can be referenced from alert annotations and the status-page update runbook. (4) Basic validation to ensure every active component in the registry has a dashboard link entry (fails CI if missing). Scope/Components: Hosted API (chat/completions), Streaming, Embeddings, Reranking, Console (incident surfacing + status endpoints), plus region views for us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1. Notes: Dedicated/Private are intentionally excluded from the public-facing component dashboard set in this PR; we can add internal-only dashboards later under a separate folder with restricted perms. Naming convention: Folder = "Status Components"; Dashboard title = "Status Component: <Display Name> (<region>)"; UID = "statuscomp-<component_id>-<region>"; Variables include component_id, region, capability (where applicable), route_group. Panels standardize on p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate (5m/30m), request volume, saturation proxy (queue depth where available), and top routes contributing to errors. Validation/rollout: This is safe to merge behind the current component registry; new components will require link entries, but the check only enforces for components marked rollout_enabled=true. Follow-ups: The anti-flap logic PR will reference these dashboards in alert annotations; the status-page update runbook will also be updated to prefer the canonical links from this repo.

title: Add JSON schema + CI validation for status component registry
preview: This PR introduces a JSON Schema for the public status-page component registry and adds validation in CI so registry changes fail fast when they are malformed or violate taxonomy rules. Motivation: we’re using the registry as a source-of-truth for component ids/display names/ownership and rollout flags as part of the Status page transparency upgrades effort; recent incidents and hotfixes showed that small typos (ids, missing fields, inconsistent region/capability labels) can cause misclassification and customer-facing status inaccuracies. What’s included: (1) New schema file `statuspage/component-registry.schema.json` that defines required fields and constraints for each component entry: `component_id` (kebab-case, stable, unique), `display_name` (human readable), `product_area` (hosted_api|streaming|embeddings|reranking|console|dedicated_control_plane), `regions` (allowed set), `capabilities` (allowed set), `ownership` (team + slack channel), and `rollout` flags (`public_enabled`, `console_link_enabled`, `auto_updates_enabled`) with defaults. (2) Validation script `scripts/validate-status-component-registry.ts` (AJV) that validates `statuspage/component-registry.json` against the schema and additionally enforces a few cross-field rules that JSON Schema can’t express cleanly (e.g., no duplicate display_name within the same product_area+region group, `public_enabled=false` components must not have `console_link_enabled=true`, and region list must be non-empty for region-scoped product areas). (3) CI wiring: adds a `statuspage-registry-validate` job to the existing checks and runs it on PRs that touch `statuspage/**`. (4) Developer ergonomics: `npm run validate:statuspage-registry` command, plus README updates describing how to add a new component safely and how to handle deprecated/renamed ids (no renames; add new id and mark old id `public_enabled=false` only after deprecation window). Notes: This does not change runtime/statuspage behavior directly; it only adds guardrails for future registry edits. Follow-ups will update incident-bot + gateway metrics tagging to rely on the validated ids (tracked in ENG-4822/ENG-4830).

title: Add KMS latency/error dashboards + alert rules (per env/region)
preview: Context: As part of the KMS integration hardening initiative we needed first-class dependency visibility for KMS across Hosted/Dedicated/Private paths. This PR adds a KMS Dependency Health dashboard and alert rules that align to the canonical error taxonomy (AccessDenied/NotFound/Throttling/Timeout/InvalidCiphertext/Unknown) and our metric namespacing conventions. Scope: (1) Dashboard: Overview + per env/region panels for request volume, latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate by category, throttling signals, and circuit breaker state; (2) Alerts: page-worthy vs ticket-worthy thresholds, with environment-aware routing (prod pages, staging tickets) and burn-rate style windows where appropriate; (3) Operator ergonomics: runbook links + log/trace pivot hints (trace_id, kms_key_id, provider, region, service); (4) Documentation: short README explaining how to use/tune the alerts and what to check first during an incident. Recommended thresholds (initial): Latency p95: warn >250ms for 10m, page >500ms for 5m (prod only); Timeout error rate: warn >0.25% for 10m, page >0.75% for 5m; Throttling: warn >0.5% for 10m, page >1.5% for 10m (with annotation to check retry storm/backpressure); AccessDenied: page on sustained >10/min for 5m (prod) because it often indicates mis-scoped IAM or a rollout regression; InvalidCiphertext: page on any sustained nonzero (>1/min for 5m) because it can indicate key mismatch/rotation cutover issues. Notes: thresholds are intentionally conservative and will be tuned post-rollout (see ENG-4828 follow-up and the alert noise discussion). This PR assumes the KMS metrics emitted by the runtime/control plane are available (redwood PR-18434 / PR-18515 / PR-18527) and uses the standardized tags: env, region, service, provider, error_category, error_code, kms_key_id (hashed).

title: Dashboards: correlate model version change rate with rollout phases and incident/regression signals
preview: Context: As part of the model metadata schema + changelog generator rollout, we now emit structured model change events (promotions, rollbacks, metadata edits) and rollout phase transitions. Support and oncall repeatedly asked for a fast way to answer: “did this latency/regression coincide with a model version promotion or rollout phase change?” This PR adds Grafana panels + recording rules to correlate: (1) model version change rate, (2) rollout phase transitions (canary/A-B/GA/rollback), and (3) incident/regression signals (5xx, timeouts, p95/p99 latency, eval-guardrail failures when present). What’s included: - New dashboard: “Model Rollout Correlation” with panels for change rate over time, rollout phase annotations, and ‘before/after’ latency + error overlays. - Update existing “LLM Serving Overview” dashboard with a compact panel showing model version change rate by deployment_mode and model_name (top 10) plus a click-through to the correlation dashboard. - Prometheus recording rules to normalize and de-cardinalize model change counters for dashboards (avoid high-cardinality labels from raw events). - Standard annotations schema for rollout phase changes (Grafana annotations sourced from Loki logs) to visualize canary/A-B boundaries. Notes/assumptions: - This depends on control-plane emitting model_change and rollout_phase_change events (redwood PR #18435 / #18458). In environments where those logs/metrics aren’t present yet, panels will render ‘No data’ but won’t break dashboards. - We intentionally do not include per-customer/account identifiers in any metric labelset; correlation for account-specific cases should use trace IDs / audit tooling, not dashboards. - Private deployments: these dashboards assume the “control-plane-events” log stream is configured; otherwise skip the annotations panel per docs added here.

What’s included: - New dashboard: “Model Rollout Correlation” with panels for change rate over time, rollout phase annotations, and ‘before/after’ latency + error overlays. - Update existing “LLM Serving Overview” dashboard with a compact panel showing model version change rate by deployment_mode and model_name (top 10) plus a click-through to the correlation dashboard. - Prometheus recording rules to normalize and de-cardinalize model change counters for dashboards (avoid high-cardinality labels from raw events). - Standard annotations schema for rollout phase changes (Grafana annotations sourced from Loki logs) to visualize canary/A-B boundaries. Notes/assumptions: - This depends on control-plane emitting model_change and rollout_phase_change events (redwood PR #18435 / #18458). In environments where those logs/metrics aren’t present yet, panels will render ‘No data’ but won’t break dashboards. - We intentionally do not include per-customer/account identifiers in any metric labelset; correlation for account-specific cases should use trace IDs / audit tooling, not dashboards. - Private deployments: these dashboards assume the “control-plane-events” log stream is configured; otherwise skip the annotations panel per docs added here.

title: Add Private minimum pack Prometheus alert rules: latency + availability (with SLO burn recording rules)
preview:
Context: This PR introduces the baseline alerting + recording rules for the Redwood Private observability minimum pack, focused on the highest-impact failure modes for inference availability and latency regressions. These are intended to be safe-by-default in customer clusters (bounded cardinality, conservative paging) and to map cleanly to the SLO guidance we’ve been using in staging.

What’s included (v1):
- Recording rules (namespace: redwood_private:*) used by dashboards + alerts so queries are consistent and cheaper.
- Latency SLO burn alerts (multi-window, multi-burn) for the API gateway and runtime request path.
- Availability alerts (5xx rate + blackbox reachability) for API ingress and control plane critical services.
- Alert annotations include runbook + first steps links and a canonical dashboard deep link placeholder.
- promtool tests for rule syntax + basic evaluation fixtures.

Alert philosophy / thresholds (rationale):
- We page only on issues that are likely customer-visible and sustained.
- Latency: burn-rate alerts use a 2-window strategy (fast burn for rapid regressions, slow burn for creeping issues). This matches Aisha’s guidance for Private: prefer burn rates over raw p95 spikes to avoid noise in heterogeneous customer traffic.
- Availability: default is request-based (5xx ratio) rather than absolute error count; includes a minimum request rate gate to avoid low-traffic false positives.
- We intentionally avoid high-cardinality labels in alerts (no tenant_id, no raw model name, no full route). Where route-level is needed, we aggregate to route_group and/or service.

Defaults (tuned for min pack):
- Page (Sev2): sustained burn likely to exhaust error budget within 1h OR API is unreachable.
- Ticket (Sev3): slower burn / partial degradation / early warning.
- Warning alerts are created but set to disabled-by-default in values; the helm wiring PR will decide final toggles.

Runbooks:
- Latency burn: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPRIV/Runbook+-+Private+Latency+SLO+Burn
- Availability / 5xx: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPRIV/Runbook+-+Private+Availability+and+5xx
- Prometheus scrape health (support): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGINFRA/Prometheus+and+Grafana+for+Private+Installations

Notes / follow-ups:
- GPU saturation, OOM, and restart-loop alerts are tracked separately (ENG-19148).
- This PR does not wire rules into helm values; that is handled in redwood-helm-charts PR-18405.
- We should revisit the latency objective (currently defaults to 800ms p95 for chat endpoints) after we have 2–3 customer baselines from validation.

Checklist:
- [x] Recording rules included for RPS, error ratio, latency quantiles, and SLI measurements
- [x] Alerts include runbook URLs and concise "what to do first" steps
- [x] promtool rule tests added
- [x] Label cardinality bounded (no per-tenant or per-model alert dimension)
- [x] Reviewed against Private metrics/alerting standards

Screenshots/Examples:
- Included in the conversation: example firing of LatencySLOBurnFast after injected delay; and non-firing for low traffic due to request rate gate.

title: Add Private minimum pack: versioned Grafana dashboard bundle (runtime + control plane + infra)
preview: Summary: This PR adds the first versioned Grafana dashboard bundle for the Private Observability Minimum Pack. The dashboards are designed to be shipped as immutable artifacts (checked into this repo) and later installed via `redwood-helm-charts`/`redwood-private-installer` (follow-ups). Scope: 1) Runtime overview dashboard focused on golden signals + inference-specific panels (token throughput, batching, KV cache, GPU utilization). 2) Control plane health dashboard (gateway/auth/policy/config services) with standard service golden signals and dependency panels (DB/redis if present). 3) Infra dashboard for customer cluster basics required to triage most common Private incidents (node pressure, PVC pressure, pod restart loops, scrape status). Design notes / constraints: - Private environments require bounded label cardinality; dashboards avoid templating on high-cardinality labels like raw `route`, `tenant_id`, `request_id`. Where filtering is useful, panels use a small set of safe dimensions (`namespace`, `service`, `cluster`, `instance`), and model/route usage is represented via top-k panels based on recording rules (to be introduced in ENG-19144/PR-18356). - Dashboards are versioned under `dashboards/private/minimum-pack/v1.0.0/` and include stable UIDs prefixed with `rw-private-` to ensure upgrades don’t create duplicates. - Panels include conservative defaults (5m/1h ranges) and annotate ‘expected exporters’ (kube-state-metrics, node-exporter, NVIDIA DCGM exporter) but do not hard-depend on optional exporters (panels degrade gracefully when metrics are missing). What’s included (v1.0.0): - `Runtime / Overview (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-runtime-overview`) - `Control Plane / Health (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-control-plane-health`) - `Private / Cluster Infra` (UID: `rw-private-cluster-infra`) - `Private / Prometheus Scrape Health` (UID: `rw-private-prom-scrape-health`) (small helper dashboard to debug ‘empty panels’ issues) Implementation details: - Adds a bundle manifest (`dashboards/private/minimum-pack/bundle.yaml`) that enumerates dashboards, UIDs, and target folder names for the installer. - Adds lightweight validation tooling in CI (`make dashboards-validate`) to ensure exported JSON is stable, has a UID, and does not embed org-specific datasource IDs. - Standardizes dashboard tags: `redwood`, `private`, `minimum-pack`, and component tag (e.g. `runtime`, `control-plane`, `infra`). Testing: - Ran `make dashboards-validate` locally. - Imported into Grafana 10.4 with a local Prometheus, verified template variables resolve and panels render with a simulated dataset. - Verified dashboards import with no ‘Datasource not found’ errors by using named datasource `Prometheus` and `Loki` placeholders (installer will map). Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Wire into `redwood-helm-charts` values + installer packaging (ENG-19152 / PR-18405). - Add recording rules and alerts referenced by some panels (ENG-19144 / PR-18356). - Add on-prem offline bundle inclusion + checksums/signing (ENG-19154 / PR-18441).

title: Add alert rules + routing for Dedicated max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup
preview:
Title: Add alert rules + routing for Dedicated max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup

Context
Dedicated autoscaling bounds are rolling out to pilot customers. During pilot we’ve seen two recurring failure/impact modes that are easy to miss without dedicated alerting:
1) Deployment hits max bound for an extended period while demand continues increasing (customers see queueing / p95 latency regressions; support tickets like SUP-28411).
2) Sustained queue buildup (even if not max-bound) indicating a scaling issue, forecast signal outage, or model-level throughput regression.

This PR adds:
- Prometheus alert rules for max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup.
- Alertmanager routing to Dedicated on-call (page) vs #eng-infra (ticket/notify) depending on severity and duration.
- Rule annotations/runbook links aligned with the Dedicated autoscaler troubleshooting runbook.

What’s included
- New alerts (names follow metric naming standards and keep labels low-cardinality):
  - DedicatedMaxBoundSustained (page): bounded at max AND queue depth elevated for >= 10m.
  - DedicatedMaxBoundHitRateHigh (notify): high ratio of bounded_desired == max over a rolling window, even if queue is not yet elevated.
  - DedicatedQueueDepthSustained (page): queue depth above threshold for >= 15m (guarded by traffic floor to avoid noise).
  - DedicatedMinBoundUnmet (notify): min bound cannot be met (region capacity shortage / provisioning failure), intended as early warning to SRE + capacity planning.
- Routing:
  - Severity=page routes to PagerDuty service "dedicated-oncall" with grouping by (deployment_id, region).
  - Severity=notify routes to Slack #eng-infra and creates a ticket via existing webhook (no new integration).
- Documentation pointers:
  - Annotations include dashboard links (from pr-1934) and runbook section anchors.

Thresholds (initial)
- Queue depth signal uses dedicated_autoscaler_queue_depth (already emitted by fleet telemetry) with a traffic floor via req_rate to avoid paging on low-volume deployments.
- Max-bound saturation uses dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas == dedicated_autoscaler_max_replicas AND queue depth above a small baseline (to avoid false positives when customers intentionally cap above demand).
- All thresholds were chosen to match pilot expectations and can be tuned post-canary; see inline comments in rules.

Files / areas changed
- alerts/dedicated/autoscaling_bounds_alerts.yaml: new rule group(s) for bounds + queueing.
- alertmanager/routes/dedicated.yaml: new routes + grouping rules.
- alertmanager/receivers/pagerduty.yaml: ensure dedicated-oncall receiver exists and has correct service key reference.
- dashboards/links.yaml: adds canonical URLs used by alert annotations.
- tests/promtool_rules_test.yaml: rule unit tests covering basic firing/non-firing cases.

Rollout plan
- Landing behind existing Dedicated bounds rollout (feature is opt-in per account), but alerting is safe to enable globally since it only evaluates when metrics exist.
- We will initially enable paging routes only for pilot accounts (via matcher on customer_tier=pilot) for 1 week, then broaden to all Dedicated once noise is acceptable.

Checklist
- [x] promtool check passes
- [x] alert rule tests added
- [x] annotations include runbook + dashboard
- [x] routing reviewed by SRE

Notes / follow-ups
- We should consider adding a separate alert for "predictive signal stale" once the predictor staleness metric is stable (tracked in ENG-22142).
- The min-bound unmet alert is notify-only by design; paging policy should be revisited after we collect baseline rate.

Screenshots
N/A (rule changes only).

title: Private minimum pack: add GPU saturation + OOM/resource pressure alerts for inference pods
preview:
Context: Private minimum pack needs a minimal, supported set of alerts that catch the highest-impact inference failure modes (GPU saturation, GPU memory pressure, OOM kills, restart loops) without overloading customer on-calls. This PR adds GPU/resource pressure alert rules scoped to Redwood inference pods and wires them into the existing private rules bundle. It includes tuning notes from the alert-noise review and enforces label/cardinality constraints for customer Prometheus.

What’s included: (1) GPU saturation alerts (warning/ticket): sustained high GPU utilization on inference pods aggregated by cluster/namespace/workload; (2) GPU memory pressure (warning): high framebuffer utilization (FB used/total) using DCGM metrics when available; (3) OOM kill alerts (critical/page): repeated OOMKilled terminations for redwood-runtime containers over a short window; (4) restart loop alerts (warning -> critical escalation): excessive restarts for runtime pods; (5) node/pod resource pressure signals (warning): Kubernetes NodeMemoryPressure for nodes running runtime pods, and container memory RSS high (guardrail) where kubelet/cadvisor metrics exist.

Noise tuning / defaults: A few alerts are intentionally shipped disabled-by-default via a `privateMinimumPack.alerts.gpu.enableExperimental` gate in values (see helm wiring in follow-up PRs) because clusters differ significantly in baseline utilization. In particular, the "HighGPUUtilization" warning threshold is conservative and requires a long `for:` to avoid flapping during bursty token workloads. The OOMKilled alert remains enabled by default (impact is high and signal is reliable). All alerts include runbook annotations with first-actions oriented toward Private support/SRE.

Metric dependencies: This assumes (a) kube-state-metrics is present for pod termination reasons and restarts, (b) node-exporter/kubelet metrics for node conditions and container memory, and (c) DCGM exporter for GPU metrics. If DCGM metrics are absent, GPU-specific alerts will stay inactive; the rules are written to avoid hard-failing eval.

Cardinality constraints: Alerts intentionally avoid `model`, `route`, `tenant`, and `request_id` labels. Aggregations are by `cluster`, `namespace`, and `workload`/`deployment` only. For DCGM, we aggregate across GPUs per node to avoid per-GPU paging; detailed per-GPU dashboards remain the place to drill in.

Testing: Validated rules load in Prometheus (promtool), unit tests cover common label sets, and smoke-test harness was run in staging-private with synthetic load + forced OOM scenarios. Included one follow-up note: customers running strict NetworkPolicies may need to allow DCGM scrape targets; that is called out in the runbook.

Follow-ups: (1) Wire `enableExperimental` toggles into `redwood-helm-charts` private values (tracked in ENG-19152); (2) Add a short troubleshooting section to the Day-2 runbook for "DCGM metrics missing" and "OOMKilled alert firing" patterns; (3) Consider a recording rule for GPU mem fraction to simplify dashboards if we keep these long-term.

Alert summary (names may change slightly before GA): - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeGPUUtilizationHigh (warning, disabled-by-default) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeGPUMemoryPressure (warning, disabled-by-default) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeOOMKilled (critical, enabled) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeRestartLoop (warning/critical, enabled) - RedwoodPrivateNodeMemoryPressureForRuntime (warning, enabled)

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (a) severity mapping (page vs ticket), (b) label usage/cardinality, (c) thresholds and `for:` durations, and (d) runbook annotation clarity (what to do first).

title: Add Private upgrade health dashboards (prechecks, migrations, error rates, rollbacks)
preview: Summary: Adds a Grafana dashboard pack for Redwood Private upgrade health, focused on the new installer-driven upgrade workflow (prechecks -> backup -> control plane rollout -> data plane rollout -> migrations -> postchecks, with rollback entry points). Dashboard set covers (1) precheck failure rates + breakdown by check_id/severity, (2) DB migration durations and timeouts/lock waits, (3) API/control-plane error rates during upgrade windows, and (4) rollback frequency + outcomes. Motivation: SRE and Release Eng asked for a single place to validate upgrade readiness and spot regressions during beta rollouts; today we rely on scattered logs and ad-hoc queries. This PR makes upgrades observable with consistent panels and variables that work in Hosted VPC Private and on-prem (including air-gapped) where only Prometheus + Loki are available. What’s included: - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Overview" (upgrade success rate, active upgrades, error budget impact proxy, rollbacks). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Prechecks" (fail rate, top failing checks, blocking vs warning, remediation link rendering). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Migrations" (p50/p95 duration by migration_name, lock wait time, failed migrations, timeouts). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Upgrade Window Errors" (HTTP 5xx/429, gRPC error codes if present, control-plane reachability, installer command failure count). - Optional annotation integration: upgrade window markers derived from audit/installer events when present; otherwise uses a manual label/annotation query. Metrics/log sources: - Prometheus metrics emitted by installer/controller-plane components: redwood_private_upgrade_runs_total, redwood_private_upgrade_precheck_results_total{check_id,severity,result}, redwood_private_upgrade_stage_duration_seconds_bucket{stage}, redwood_db_migration_duration_seconds_bucket{migration}, redwood_db_migration_failures_total{migration,reason}, redwood_private_rollback_runs_total{result}. - Loki log-derived counters for older installs missing a subset of metrics (best-effort fallback queries). Installation notes: Dashboards are templated with common labels (cluster, environment, customer, deployment_mode). On-prem guidance included: set prometheus external_labels for customer + cluster; if unavailable, dashboards still work but variables are limited. Screenshots: attached in PR (Overview + Prechecks + Migrations). Risks/rollout: purely additive dashboards/queries; no alert changes in this PR. Follow-ups: add alerts for migration timeout and elevated rollback frequency once beta thresholds stabilize; wire in audit event fields after redwood/pr-29187 lands everywhere.

Dashboard set covers (1) precheck failure rates + breakdown by check_id/severity, (2) DB migration durations and timeouts/lock waits, (3) API/control-plane error rates during upgrade windows, and (4) rollback frequency + outcomes. Motivation: SRE and Release Eng asked for a single place to validate upgrade readiness and spot regressions during beta rollouts; today we rely on scattered logs and ad-hoc queries. This PR makes upgrades observable with consistent panels and variables that work in Hosted VPC Private and on-prem (including air-gapped) where only Prometheus + Loki are available. What’s included: - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Overview" (upgrade success rate, active upgrades, error budget impact proxy, rollbacks). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Prechecks" (fail rate, top failing checks, blocking vs warning, remediation link rendering). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Migrations" (p50/p95 duration by migration_name, lock wait time, failed migrations, timeouts). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Upgrade Window Errors" (HTTP 5xx/429, gRPC error codes if present, control-plane reachability, installer command failure count). - Optional annotation integration: upgrade window markers derived from audit/installer events when present; otherwise uses a manual label/annotation query. Metrics/log sources: - Prometheus metrics emitted by installer/controller-plane components: redwood_private_upgrade_runs_total, redwood_private_upgrade_precheck_results_total{check_id,severity,result}, redwood_private_upgrade_stage_duration_seconds_bucket{stage}, redwood_db_migration_duration_seconds_bucket{migration}, redwood_db_migration_failures_total{migration,reason}, redwood_private_rollback_runs_total{result}. - Loki log-derived counters for older installs missing a subset of metrics (best-effort fallback queries). Installation notes: Dashboards are templated with common labels (cluster, environment, customer, deployment_mode). On-prem guidance included: set prometheus external_labels for customer + cluster; if unavailable, dashboards still work but variables are limited. Screenshots: attached in PR (Overview + Prechecks + Migrations). Risks/rollout: purely additive dashboards/queries; no alert changes in this PR. Follow-ups: add alerts for migration timeout and elevated rollback frequency once beta thresholds stabilize; wire in audit event fields after redwood/pr-29187 lands everywhere.

title: Add Audit Exporter Grafana dashboards + alert rules (lag, delivery errors)
preview: Context: As part of the SIEM-ready audit export beta, we need first-class operational visibility for the audit-log-exporter service (lag, retries, adapter failures) and alerting aligned to the SLOs defined by SRE. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and Prometheus alert rules for the exporter, intended to be deployed to hosted + dedicated environments for the beta cohort. Links: Linear ENG-5168 (exporter observability), Confluence SLO doc: "Audit exporter SLOs and alerting" (owner: Sean/Rafael). Scope: (1) Grafana dashboard: Overview (lag, throughput, success rate), (2) Grafana dashboard: Destination adapters (Splunk HEC / Datadog / S3), (3) Alert rules: export lag burn, sustained adapter errors, DLQ growth, auto-paused exports, (4) Recording rules for rate-based panels to reduce query cost. Notes on semantics: Export lag is computed from exporter checkpoint watermark vs event time; alerts use p95 lag by tenant and also a global max with cardinality controls. We intentionally avoid alerting on single-tenant spikes unless they violate the global budget or a configured "beta tenant allowlist" label is present. Rollout: safe to merge; rules are gated behind the existing audit-exporter job selector and will be enabled per cluster by SRE during beta rollout.

title: Add onboarding KPIs dashboard (TTFS/TTFP + checklist funnel) and recommended alerts
preview:
## Summary
Adds a packaged Grafana dashboard + alert rules for the Guided Onboarding / First Production Launch initiative.

This is intended to be imported via the standard `observability-pack` rollout (same mechanism as the latency/cost packs), and targets the onboarding telemetry datasets produced by the gateway/control plane eventing work + derived milestone tables.

## What’s included
Dashboard: **Onboarding KPIs (Hosted API)**
- TTFS (signup → first successful 2xx request) trend
  - Median / p90 / p95 by signup cohort
  - Breakdown by `sdk_language` (via user-agent tags), `region`, and `plan_tier`
- TTFP (signup → first production traffic) trend
  - Median / p90 by signup cohort
  - Includes optional filter for "prod_traffic" classifier (see notes below)
- Step completion funnel
  - % of tenants completing each checklist step (ordered)
  - Step-to-next-step conversion + time-in-step
  - Top drop-off steps (ranked)
- Drop-off reasons / first failure signals
  - First auth failure (401/403) rate by cohort
  - First 429 rate by cohort
  - First streaming disconnect signal rate (from gateway termination reasons)
- Data quality tiles
  - Event ingest freshness (minutes behind)
  - Missing required dimensions rate (null/unknown sdk_language/region)

Alerts (recommended; shipped disabled-by-default)
- **Onboarding KPI dataset freshness**
  - Triggers when `onboarding_events_v1` ingestion is > 30m behind for 2 consecutive evaluation windows
  - Also triggers when derived `tenant_onboarding_milestones` hasn’t updated in > 60m
- **Step drop-off spike**
  - Triggers when a step’s completion rate drops > 20% vs trailing 7d baseline (configurable)
  - Default scope is Hosted API + self-serve cohorts; enterprise/dedicated cohorts can be configured separately

## Data sources and metric definitions
This pack assumes the following tables/views exist in the central analytics workspace (names match the platform telemetry spec as of March 2025):
- `analytics.onboarding_events_v1` (raw / lightly normalized events)
- `analytics.tenant_onboarding_milestones_v1` (derived; 1 row per tenant with timestamps per step)

Metric definitions are aligned to PM-751:
- TTFS: `first_success_at - signup_at`
  - `first_success_at` is the first gateway 2xx for the canonical generate/chat endpoints, excluding health checks
- TTFP: `first_prod_traffic_at - signup_at`
  - `first_prod_traffic_at` is computed by downstream classifier (>= N requests over M minutes OR explicit "prod" tag). The dashboard supports both modes via a variable.

Important: the dashboard does **not** join to billing for these KPIs (to avoid the known usage dashboard lag issue). We only use billing-derived fields when available as optional breakdown dimensions.

## How to install
- `obs-pack apply onboarding-kpis`
- or import the JSON from `packs/onboarding-kpis/dashboards/onboarding_kpis_grafana.json`

After import, set variables:
- `environment` (prod/staging)
- `cohort_window` (default: last 8 weeks)
- `deployment_mode` (default: hosted)

## Screenshots
Attached in internal PR UI (Grafana panels: funnel + TTFS/TTFP timeseries + alert preview).

## Notes / limitations
- Step drop-off alerting uses a simple baseline comparison. This is intentionally conservative for MVP; we can add seasonality-aware detection once we see stable weekly traffic.
- `sdk_language` depends on OpenAI-compat user-agent tagging (redwood-openai-compat#265). If not present, values show as `unknown`.
- This PR ships Hosted API defaults; Dedicated/Private filters are stubbed but not surfaced in the default dashboard nav.

## Testing
- Validated queries against staging analytics (last 14d) and ensured panels render with empty-state handling.
- Dry-run the alert rules with `alerts.test_mode=true` to confirm routing/labels.
- Checked for cardinality regressions (no per-tenant series in alerts; tenant-level only in dashboard table panels).

## Rollout plan
- Merge behind pack version bump only (no automatic enablement).
- SRE/Telemetry will enable alerts for internal dogfood cohorts first (see ENG-5815).

## Checklist
- [x] Dashboard uses standard variable names and folder layout
- [x] Alerts are disabled by default and include runbook links
- [x] No PII fields referenced (tenant_id is only used in table panels and not emitted in alert labels)
- [x] Added docs for metric definitions and known caveats

title: Alert tuning: GPU saturation + queue depth (reduce noise, keep early warning)
preview:
Context: Over the last ~6 weeks we’ve had repeated pages for brief GPU util spikes and transient scheduler queue growth (especially during deploy waves / regional rebalancing). These pages are noisy, but we still want early warning when we’re genuinely saturating and heading toward latency/5xx SLO burn. This PR tunes the GPU saturation + queue depth alert rules used by Hosted and Dedicated pools, aligning them with the standardized saturation signals that incident-bot v2 surfaces.

What’s changing (high level): (1) GPU util paging now requires persistence and corroboration (queue depth or admission/backpressure) instead of firing on short 1–2 minute spikes. (2) Added a warning tier for early detection (route/regional) and kept a stricter paging tier for sustained saturation. (3) Queue depth alerting is now based on a smoothed p95 + “time-above-threshold” gate and avoids firing during known noisy windows (autoscaler warm-up / drain) via an explicit label-based suppression rule. (4) Improved annotations: alert messages now include consistent deep links (saturation overview, scheduler queue dashboard, error budget burn link generator) and show the key dimensions incident-bot cards rely on (service, region, pool, capacity_tier).

Details: - GPU saturation: switched from avg(gpu_util) > 90% for 5m (page) to a two-tier approach. Warning: gpu_util_p95 > 85% for 10m AND scheduler_queue_depth_p95 > 0 (or admission_backpressure_rate > 0) with severity=warning. Page: gpu_util_p95 > 92% for 20m AND scheduler_queue_depth_p95 > 5 for 10m, scoped to pool_type in {hosted,dedicated} and excluding canary pools by default. - Queue depth: removed the previous “queue_depth > 10 for 5m” page (too spiky / low signal). New: warning on queue_depth_p95 > 8 for 10m, page on queue_depth_p95 > 15 for 15m with an additional condition that request_rate is non-trivial (to avoid alerting during low-traffic drift). - Suppression/inhibition: added inhibition so we don’t page on saturation if the region is in an active incident/maintenance annotation (existing label convention region_state=draining|maintenance), and so queue depth pages are inhibited when a higher-severity GPU saturation page is already firing for the same (service, region, pool).

Why these thresholds: based on the signal inventory + on-call feedback from recent incidents where responders found queue depth and KV cache pressure more actionable than raw GPU util. We also looked at the last 20 saturation-related pages and found >50% resolved in <7 minutes without mitigation; those should be warnings and/or show up in dashboards, not wake people up.

Testing/validation: - promtool rule tests added for key scenarios (spike vs sustained, low-traffic, canary pools). - backtested against 14 days of metrics in us-east + eu-west using the alert preview in Console; expected paging volume reduction ~30–40% while still firing during the known sustained saturation episodes (eu-west capacity event and two dedicated pool crunches).

Rollout plan: merge + ship with next observability-pack sync; SRE will monitor alert volume for 1 week and adjust if we see missed early warnings. This is part of the Incident Bot + diagnostics improvements initiative; the bot’s saturation card links/annotations expect these labels and dashboards.

Related: ENG-5423 (saturation signals into incident-bot), ENG-5424 (error budget burn links), INT-3341 (telemetry query timeouts led us to prefer stable aggregations), and the saturation signals standardization doc.

title: Add rollout cohort panels (latency + errors) and alert templates
preview:
Context: As part of Hosted canary rollout GA work, we need a standardized dashboard/alert baseline that makes it easy to compare baseline vs canary cohorts (latency + errors) without every team re-deriving PromQL and without blowing up cardinality. This PR adds a small set of reusable panels + a “rollout overview” dashboard to the observability pack, plus alert templates and recommended default thresholds for auto-stop/rollback wiring. What’s included: (1) New dashboard: “Hosted Rollouts / Cohort Comparison (Latency + Errors)” with panels for request rate, error rate, timeout rate, p50/p95 latency, and a baseline-vs-canary delta view. Panels are parameterized around {route, region, rollout_id} and assume cohort in {baseline, canary}. (2) Library panels (Jsonnet) that can be embedded into other service dashboards: p95 latency by cohort, 5xx rate by cohort, timeouts by cohort, and “canary/baseline ratio” panels (ratio and absolute delta). (3) Alert templates (PrometheusRule) for: a) canary 5xx rate regression (absolute + ratio guard), b) canary timeout regression, c) canary p95 latency regression, and d) “metrics missing / cohort tag missing” signal during an active rollout. Templates intentionally do NOT page by default; they are wired as warning-level with a short runbook link and intended to be used by the rollout orchestrator’s stop conditions / SRE playbooks. (4) Notes on cardinality and query safety: queries avoid grouping on rollout_id by default; most panels are scoped via dashboard variables and have explicit max series safeguards. Where we do present per-rollout series, we restrict via regex variable and add a “topk(20, …)” in examples. Implementation details: Metrics assumed (aligned with data plane tagging work) are: redwood_gateway_requests_total{route,region,cohort,rollout_id,status_class}, redwood_gateway_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route,region,cohort,rollout_id}, redwood_gateway_timeouts_total{route,region,cohort,rollout_id}. If your environment uses the older naming (rw_gateway_*), the dashboard variables include a compatibility toggle; see README section below. PromQL patterns: error_rate = sum(rate(requests_total{status_class=~"5.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total[5m])); latency_p95 = histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le,cohort) (rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))); regression guards use a combination of ratio (canary/baseline) and absolute delta to avoid false positives at low traffic. Suggested defaults (documented, not enforced): 5xx regression: canary_5xx_rate > baseline_5xx_rate + 0.5% AND canary/baseline > 1.5 for 5m with min canary RPS >= 0.2; timeouts: canary_timeout_rate > baseline_timeout_rate + 0.2% AND ratio > 2.0 for 5m with min canary RPS >= 0.2; p95 latency: canary_p95 > baseline_p95 + 250ms AND ratio > 1.25 for 10m with min canary RPS >= 0.2. These match current SRE beta tuning guidance and should be revisited before broad GA (see follow-ups). Files changed (high-level): grafana/dashboards/hosted_rollouts_cohort_comparison.jsonnet; grafana/lib/panels/rollout_cohort_latency.libsonnet; grafana/lib/panels/rollout_cohort_errors.libsonnet; prometheus/alerts/rollouts_cohort_regressions.yaml; prometheus/alerts/rollouts_metrics_missing.yaml; docs/rollouts/COHORT_DASHBOARDS.md; mixin/rollouts-mixin.jsonnet; mixin/rollouts-mixin.libsonnet; scripts/render_dashboards.sh (small updates). Screenshots: Included in docs/rollouts/COHORT_DASHBOARDS.md (rendered examples for us-east and eu-west). Rollout plan: ship in observability-pack v0.31.0; Redwood monorepo will vendor this version as part of the canary GA release checklist. Testing: jsonnet fmt + jsonnet render validation; promtool check rules; Grafana dashboard JSON schema validation in CI. Follow-ups (not in this PR): (a) Add a small “quality metrics” section when those signals become stable; (b) Expand panels for tokens/sec and cost per 1k tokens once telemetry joins those dimensions; (c) Confirm final threshold defaults after beta exit data is compiled.

Commits: 1) mixin(rollouts): add cohort comparison dashboard shell and variables 2) panels(rollouts): add latency cohort panels + delta/ratio views 3) panels(rollouts): add errors + timeouts cohort panels 4) alerts(rollouts): add regression templates + missing-metrics rule 5) docs(rollouts): cohort dashboard usage + cardinality guidance 6) ci: validate rendered dashboards + promtool checks

Review notes / conversation: Allison Grant: “Please make sure the default dashboard doesn’t accidentally enumerate rollout_id across tenants; require explicit variable selection.” -> Updated panels to avoid grouping by rollout_id by default, and made rollout_id variable opt-in with a strict regex + helper text. Thomas Baird: “The histogram_quantile query is missing a sum by (le) in one panel; also please keep the range window consistent (5m) unless we have a reason.” -> Fixed p95 query and standardized default windows; left p95 regression alert at 10m with rationale in comments. Sean Gallagher: “Threshold defaults look aggressive for low-traffic routes. Can we bake in a minimum RPS gate?” -> Added min canary RPS clause and documented it in docs; also changed alert severity to warning by default. Logan Wright: “Metric names differ between hosted gateway and data plane; please add a compatibility note.” -> Added docs note and a dashboard variable toggle for legacy names.

CI: Initial run failed promtool due to YAML indentation in rollouts_metrics_missing.yaml; fixed and re-ran. Final checks: jsonnet-lint PASS, render-dashboards PASS, promtool PASS, unit PASS.

Related: This is intended to be consumed alongside redwood PR-28819 (telemetry tagging rollout_id/cohort) and redwood PR-28910 (dashboards/alerts templates in monorepo); this PR provides the shared baseline pack versioned separately.

title: Add Grafana dashboard: Route Experiment Guardrails (latency/error/cost + attribution drop)
preview:
## Summary This PR adds a standard Grafana dashboard for Route-level Experiments guardrails and data quality signals. The dashboard is intended to be deployed alongside the route experiments beta/GA so on-call and customers (where applicable) can quickly answer: (1) Is any variant regressing on latency, error rate, or cost? (2) Are we missing experiment attribution (drop rate) such that results are untrustworthy? (3) Is traffic split behaving as expected (e.g., variant getting ~0% traffic)?

This dashboard complements the control-plane/Console views by providing a fast, infrastructure-centric view over raw telemetry, and is designed to work with the canonical attribution dimensions from ADR-048 (route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region, model_id) while limiting cardinality blast-radius.

## What’s included ### Dashboard: "Route Experiments / Guardrails" Panels (high-level): - Overview row: total RPS, total requests, error rate (5xx + 429 separately), and attribution coverage.
- Latency guardrails: p50/p95/p99 request latency by variant (timeseries + stat) with a "% delta vs control" table.
- Error guardrails: error rate by variant (5xx and 429 broken out), plus "top error codes" if available.
- Cost guardrails: $/1K output tokens and $/request by variant (based on Redwood cost attribution metrics).
- Throughput: tokens/sec and requests/sec by variant (useful for distinguishing capacity issues from model behavior).
- Traffic split sanity: % of traffic per variant; includes a "variant ~0 traffic" panel to catch allocation/eligibility mistakes.
- Data quality: experiment attribution drop rate (requests missing experiment_id/variant_id when they should be present), plus a "streaming retry attribution" hint panel (links to runbook section).

### Template variables - env (prod/staging) - region - route_id (normalized route template id; NOT raw path) - experiment_id - variant_id - model_id (optional; default All)

### Data sources / metric expectations This dashboard assumes the router/runtime emits experiment dimensions on key metrics and/or spans, as implemented in the core monorepo (see redwood PRs: request context plumbing + OTel tagging). The queries here are written to tolerate partial rollout by falling back to "All variants" if variant tags are absent.

Primary metrics used (names reflect current conventions in the pack; see README changes for exact mapping): - redwood_request_latency_ms_bucket{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_requests_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region, status_class} - redwood_tokens_output_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_cost_usd_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_experiment_attribution_missing_total{route_id, region, reason} and redwood_requests_total (used as denominator)

## Why / context - We’ve had multiple beta issues where Console results were confusing or appeared stale, and on-call needed a quick way to validate (a) attribution integrity and (b) whether a regression was real vs. a data artifact (ref: SUP-24910).
- SRE asked for a standardized dashboard that can be installed for every region/environment with consistent panels and variable naming so runbooks/alerts can deep-link reliably.
- The attribution drop rate panels are explicitly included because missing experiment_id/variant_id has been a recurring failure mode (streaming retries, async spans, and header/sticky-key issues).

## Implementation notes - Cardinality: the dashboard does NOT group by raw request path. It uses normalized route_id (template) and requires users to select an experiment_id for most by-variant panels. We avoid “group by {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}” in single queries unless the user is already scoped down.
- Cost metrics: panels use $/1K output tokens and $/request. This avoids misleading comparisons for variants with different output length distributions.
- Control variant: the “delta vs control” table assumes variant_id="control" when present; otherwise it picks the lowest-sorted variant_id as a fallback. This is called out in the panel description so it doesn’t silently mislead.

## Changed files - grafana/dashboards/route-experiments/route-experiments-guardrails.jsonnet: new dashboard definition - grafana/dashboards/route-experiments/route-experiments-guardrails.dashboard.json: rendered dashboard - grafana/dashboards/route-experiments/README.md: usage notes, required labels, variable conventions - grafana/library-panels/latency-percentiles-by-variant.jsonnet: extracted reusable panel - grafana/library-panels/attribution-drop-rate.jsonnet: extracted reusable panel - CHANGELOG.md: entry for the new dashboard

## Screenshots (described) - "Latency (p95/p99) by variant" timeseries: color-coded variants, with control highlighted - "Attribution drop rate" stat: shows % missing attribution + reason breakdown in a table

## Test plan - Rendered dashboards via jsonnet build (make dashboards) - Validated PromQL in staging against synthetic traffic + a known experiment_id (confirmed series appear with expected labels) - Verified variable dropdowns do not time out when unscoped (route_id and experiment_id are optional until selecting per-variant panels)

## Follow-ups (not in this PR) - Alerts live in a separate PR (pr-1942) to keep review surface smaller and allow independent tuning.
- Add runbook deep links once URLs are finalized (placeholder links included as annotations for now).

## Checklist - [x] Dashboard renders (jsonnet + rendered json committed) - [x] Variables align with attribution schema (route_id/experiment_id/variant_id/region) - [x] Panel descriptions call out assumptions (control selection, partial rollout behavior) - [x] Changelog entry added

title: Add ingestion-side cardinality guards for cache telemetry
preview:
## Summary
This PR implements ingestion-side cardinality guards for the new prefix/KV cache telemetry emitted by the runtime and tagged by the gateway. The goal is to prevent accidental series explosions (per-tenant/per-route/per-request-shape label combinations) while preserving the ability to break down cache signals by the dimensions we actually operate on (model, route, deployment mode, region, and prompt-length/token bins).

This is the ingestion counterpart to runtime tagging + allowlists (see redwood/pr-18426) and ensures that even if a client or misconfigured gateway emits unexpected label keys/values, we either (a) rewrite to a safe aggregated bucket, or (b) drop with explicit counters so we can diagnose.

## Context / Motivation
During early canary of cache telemetry dashboards we saw a few failure modes:
- `tenant_id` and raw `route` occasionally showing up due to legacy gateway mappings -> high cardinality.
- model/version fields not consistently normalized -> multiple spellings causing unnecessary series multiplication.
- prompt/token histogram tags are safe only when bucketed; unbucketed token counts are not.

This PR codifies the policy from the cache metrics standards doc and makes the enforcement explicit in the ingestion pipeline. It also addresses SUP-3927 (customer saw "zero hits" because the old temporary guard was dropping whole samples without surfacing why).

## What this PR does
- Adds a cache-telemetry-specific label guard stage in the metrics ingestion pipeline (remote-write receiver path).
- Enforces an allowlist of label keys for cache metrics (by metric name prefix `redwood_cache_*`).
- Enforces normalization/rewrites for a subset of label values:
  - `deployment_mode`: normalize to `hosted|dedicated|private|unknown`
  - `region`: normalize to our region IDs; unknown -> `unknown`
  - `model_family` / `model`: require match to known catalog pattern; unknown -> `other`
  - `model_version`: if missing or malformed -> `unknown` (do not attempt to parse arbitrary hashes)
  - `route_id`: allow only gateway-generated stable IDs (format `rt_*`); otherwise -> `unknown`
  - `prompt_len_bucket`, `prefill_tokens_bucket`, `decode_tokens_bucket`: must be one of the configured buckets; otherwise -> `overflow`
- Drops samples when disallowed label keys are present (e.g. `tenant_id`, `user_id`, `request_id`, `prompt_hash`, `raw_route`) rather than attempting to partially salvage (to avoid implicit joins that look valid but aren’t).
- Adds explicit ingestion visibility metrics:
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_dropped_samples_total{reason,metric}`
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_rewritten_samples_total{rewrite,metric}`
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_active{guard="cache_cardinality"}=1`
- Adds a small dashboard snippet (panel-only) for guard drop/rewrites so SRE can quickly determine whether a hit-rate dip is real vs missing telemetry.

## Policy encoded (high level)
- Cache metrics are allowed to be sliced by: `model`, `model_family`, `model_version` (coarse), `route_id` (stable), `deployment_mode`, `region`, and bucketed request shape dimensions.
- Cache metrics are NOT allowed to include per-tenant labels at ingestion. Tenant/route/customer views must be served via analytics aggregates or controlled rollups, not raw high-cardinality metrics.

## Implementation notes
- Guard runs as part of the ingest pipeline stage `LabelGuardProcessor` before relabeling + storage write.
- Rules are applied only to a configured metric name prefix list (currently cache metrics only). Non-cache metrics are unaffected.
- Drop vs rewrite strategy:
  - Disallowed label keys -> drop sample (reason: `forbidden_label_key`)
  - Missing required labels -> rewrite to `unknown` where safe; do not drop (reason: `missing_required_label` increments rewrite counter)
  - Out-of-range bucket values -> rewrite to `overflow`

## Tests
- Unit tests for:
  - forbidden keys cause drops + correct reason counter
  - value normalization + rewrite counters
  - bucket enforcement
  - metric scoping (only applies to `redwood_cache_*`)
- Golden tests for a few representative runtime metric payloads (KV hit/miss, prefix reuse histograms, eviction pressure gauges).

## Rollout / Safety
- Default enabled in staging; prod rollout is controlled via values.yaml flag `ingest.guards.cacheCardinality.enabled`.
- Adds a hard cap for drop logging to avoid log storms.
- Includes a runbook note link in the dashboard panel description.

## Follow-ups
- Align with gateway migration plan to ensure `route_id` is always stable and backfilled for older routes (ENG-4823).
- Consider adding a controlled, sampled per-tenant view in analytics for Dedicated customers (separate effort).

## Screenshots
N/A (panel stub only; full dashboard work is in pr-612).

## Commits (squash summary)
- ingest: add cache metric label allowlist + enforcement hooks
- ingest: add drop/rewrite counters and reasons
- charts: wire guard config flags + defaults for staging
- tests: add unit/golden coverage for cache guard
- dashboards: add guard health panel stub

## Changed areas / files (high level)
- services/metrics-ingest/guard/cache_guard.go
- services/metrics-ingest/guard/cache_guard_test.go
- services/metrics-ingest/config/guards.yaml
- charts/metrics-ingest/values.yaml
- charts/metrics-ingest/templates/configmap.yaml
- dashboards/cache/cache-telemetry-guard-overview.json

---
## Review conversation (highlights)
**Nadia Rahman:** Can we avoid dropping the whole sample when a forbidden key exists? Maybe strip it and continue.
**Caleb Johnson (author):** I’d prefer drop. Stripping silently makes the metric look valid but changes join semantics; also it encourages accidental leakage. Added explicit counters + panel so we can detect and fix upstream quickly.

**Allison Grant:** Please add a single panel we can pin to the cache dashboards to answer "are we dropping cache telemetry right now?"
**Caleb:** Added `cache-telemetry-guard-overview` panel with drop/rewrite rate and top reasons.

**Sean Gallagher:** Make sure unknown region/model collapse doesn’t mask real regressions.
**Caleb:** Agree; rewrites are only for malformed values, not for legitimate new regions/models. Also emitting rewritten counters so we can see when we’re collapsing.

**Logan Wright:** Need a specific reason for SUP-3927: label-drop misconfig caused all cache metrics to disappear.
**Caleb:** Added reason taxonomy and ensured we never drop due to "missing model_version" — we rewrite to `unknown` so the metric still shows up.

Approvals:
- Nadia Rahman: Approved (requested dashboard panel; addressed)
- Allison Grant: Approved
- Sean Gallagher: Approved (rollout flag + visibility metrics)
- Logan Wright: Approved

## CI
- unit-tests: pass
- lint: pass
- helm-template: pass
- integration-smoke (staging): pass

Merged via squash to `main`.

title: Add standardized incident-context dashboards (deploy timeline, saturation overview, error budget burn)
preview: This PR adds a standardized set of dashboards intended to be linked directly from Incident Bot v2 context cards. The goal is to make the bot output actionable in the first 5 minutes by providing consistent deep links (per service/region/route/time window) for deploy correlation, saturation triage, and SLO/error-budget burn. The dashboards are designed to be safe/default-bounded (limited time range defaults, low-cardinality variables, and guardrails around tenant labels) and to work across Hosted + Dedicated pools where the standard metrics exist. Included dashboards: (1) Deploy Timeline (service/region) — deploy events overlay (canary vs full rollout), build SHA, and error/latency overlays; (2) Saturation Overview (pool/region) — GPU util, queue depth, KV cache pressure, scheduler backpressure/admission, and request concurrency; (3) Error Budget Burn (service/route/region) — 1h/6h burn rates, SLO indicator panels, and links back to the SLO Toolkit generator. Deep-link contract: all dashboards accept variables service, region, env, route (optional), pool (optional), and incident_window (defaults to last 60m). This PR intentionally does not add new metrics; it assumes the standardized saturation metrics and deploy events exist (or will be rolled out) and focuses on consistent visualization + linkability. Follow-ups: we may need to adjust recording rules once the bot beta cohort expands and we get real-world query load; also expect small iterations after the bot UX card text is finalized (panel names are aligned with current card headings).

title: Alerts: experiment config propagation lag + attribution anomalies (route experiments)
preview: Context: Route-level experiments rely on control-plane config propagation into the routing/policy layer. During beta we observed occasional propagation lag and a few classes of attribution anomalies (missing experiment_id/variant_id tags, variant receiving ~0 traffic due to config/eligibility issues, and split skew early in rollout). This PR adds Prometheus/Alertmanager rules and annotations for the standard route experiments dashboards, with SRE-reviewed runbook links and recommended severities. Scope: (1) propagation lag/staleness alerts tied to the new propagation lag metric emitted by the redwood monorepo, (2) attribution drop-rate and missing-dimension alerts for experiment metrics/traces aggregation, (3) “variant 0 traffic” detection to catch misconfigured allocations or eligibility rules. Notes: rules are written to avoid cardinality blow-ups (no per-route fanout by default); high-cardinality drilldowns remain in Grafana dashboards. Related: redwood PR-28631 (adds config propagation lag metric), redwood PR-28734 (runbook URLs in alert payloads), observability-pack PR-1934 (dashboards). Threads: slack/eng-sre/1736455521-alert-thresholds-experiment-propagation-lag.json and slack/eng-sre/1738362044-attribution-drop-rate-alert-noise.json informed thresholds/windows. text: Context: Route-level experiments rely on control-plane config propagation into the routing/policy layer. During beta we observed occasional propagation lag and a few classes of attribution anomalies (missing experiment_id/variant_id tags, variant receiving ~0 traffic due to config/eligibility issues, and split skew early in rollout). This PR adds Prometheus/Alertmanager rules and annotations for the standard route experiments dashboards, with SRE-reviewed runbook links and recommended severities. Scope: (1) propagation lag/staleness alerts tied to the new propagation lag metric emitted by the redwood monorepo, (2) attribution drop-rate and missing-dimension alerts for experiment metrics/traces aggregation, (3) “variant 0 traffic” detection to catch misconfigured allocations or eligibility rules. Notes: rules are written to avoid cardinality blow-ups (no per-route fanout by default); high-cardinality drilldowns remain in Grafana dashboards. Related: redwood PR-28631 (adds config propagation lag metric), redwood PR-28734 (runbook URLs in alert payloads), observability-pack PR-1934 (dashboards). title: Add recommended alert rules + SLO examples for Quickstart Template apps
preview: Context: As part of the Quickstart Templates launch, we want to ship a recommended alerting baseline that users can enable without getting paged to death. This PR adds an opt-in alert rule group for template apps (RAG/agents) plus example SLOs and burn-rate alerts. The rules are designed around the metrics emitted by the templates + OTel collector configs from PR-156/158, and they intentionally avoid high-cardinality dimensions (no user_id, prompt hash, raw path, or tool args). What’s included: (1) SLO examples: availability + latency SLO recording rules with suggested targets and notes on how to adapt per workload; (2) Alert rules: multi-window burn-rate (fast/slow) alerts for the SLOs, plus a small set of “hard failures” (collector down, exporter errors) and “symptom alerts” (tool failure spikes, streaming disconnect spikes, retrieval empty-result spikes) with conservative defaults; (3) Noise avoidance guidance: documentation on paging vs ticketing alerts, minimum traffic gating, for/hold durations, and recommended grouping to avoid alert storms during deploys/traffic spikes. Rollout: Alerts are opt-in via the template dashboards pack jsonnet entrypoint; we’re not enabling anything by default in the published Grafana Cloud pack. Related: ENG-4184 (observability pack integration), ENG-4186 (smoke tests incl. streaming stability), and the templates’ error taxonomy work (draft).
